docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: seniority_final is a high-precision but low-recall junior detector. When it labels a row as entry, that row is usually genuinely junior by YOE. But it leaves most junior-like postings in unknown, so it cannot be used as the only measure of entry-level share. The native LinkedIn label is also unstable across snapshots: arshkon entry rows are much less junior by YOE than scraped LinkedIn entry rows.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
I audited the LinkedIn English/date-ok frame only: 1,186,281 rows total, including 59,972 SWE rows, 22,279 SWE-adjacent rows, and 151,267 control rows. The seniority audit used SWE rows, with native-label comparisons restricted to arshkon and scraped LinkedIn because those are the comparable LinkedIn sources.

Checks run:

  1. seniority_final_source composition by source and period.
  2. seniority_final vs seniority_native confusion matrices and Cohen's kappa on arshkon and scraped LinkedIn.
  3. Native-label quality via the YOE distribution of seniority_native = 'entry' rows.
  4. Junior-share comparison under seniority_final, arshkon-only seniority_native, and the label-independent YOE proxy (yoe_extracted <= 2), plus a no-aggregator sensitivity.
  5. A 100-row LLM-routed spot-check. Only 16 rows in the SWE corpus had the requested weak title-level markers (I/II/III, L3, Level 4, etc.), so I inspected all 16 plus 84 additional LLM-routed rows. The weak-marker subset is the telling part here.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
Metric Arshkon (2024-04) Asaniczka (2024-01) Scraped LinkedIn (2026-03) Scraped LinkedIn (2026-04) seniority_final = entry share 3.73% 0.87% 3.35% 2.18% yoe_extracted <= 2 share 14.98% 10.36% 17.38% 16.97% seniority_final = unknown share 0.2% 28.2% 44.9% 50.3%

The production label is very conservative. It identifies a small set of clearly junior rows, but it leaves roughly half of 2026 scraped LinkedIn SWE postings as unknown.

The native baseline is not a clean junior benchmark. Among seniority_native = 'entry' rows with YOE:

Source n Mean YOE Median YOE yoe <= 2 arshkon 458 4.18 3.0 28.6% scraped LinkedIn 1,659 2.36 2.0 81.0%

That is a large cross-snapshot shift in the meaning of the native entry label.

seniority_final = entry rows themselves are much cleaner:

Period n Mean YOE Median YOE yoe <= 2 2024-04 65 2.05 2.0 78.5% 2024-01 77 2.52 2.0 63.6% 2026-03 138 2.03 2.0 80.4% 2026-04 81 1.84 2.0 80.2%

So the model is not hallucinating junior roles. It is mostly missing them.

Agreement with native labels is weak:

Subset n Cohen's kappa Exact accuracy arshkon raw 3,283 0.175 44.3% arshkon canonicalized (intern -> entry, executive -> director) 3,283 0.184 45.0% scraped LinkedIn raw 31,481 0.108 40.3% scraped LinkedIn canonicalized 31,481 0.131 41.9%

The canonicalization hardly changes the result. The disagreement is not just legacy-label noise.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
Two things matter here.

First, the arshkon native entry bucket is not junior in YOE terms. Its median YOE is 3.0 and only 28.6% are at <= 2 years. Scraped LinkedIn native entry rows are much cleaner. That is strong evidence that the native classifier changed semantics across snapshots.

Second, the LLM-routed weak-marker subset is almost entirely unresolved: 15 of the 16 weak-marker rows stayed unknown, with only one mid-senior label. This is not a routing catastrophe so much as a coverage limit. Titles with explicit numeric level codes exist, but the production seniority system leaves them mostly unlabeled.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
seniority_final = entry is strong on precision, weak on recall. The YOE profile of final entry rows is coherent across periods, and the no-aggregator sensitivity barely moves the entry shares. That makes the final label usable as a high-confidence junior subset.

But the junior-share story does not hold up if we use it as the main estimator of entry prevalence. The YOE proxy says junior-like postings are around 15-17% of SWE rows, while seniority_final says 2-4%. The 2024-to-2026 change under seniority_final is smaller than the arshkon-vs-asaniczka gap under the YOE proxy, so the direction is not clearly above instrument noise.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text:
• RQ1, employer-side restructuring: weakened for the junior-share component. The data do not support a simple "junior share fell" claim if the YOE proxy is treated as the primary junior measure.
  • RQ2, task and requirement migration: still plausible, but junior-level claims need a better measurement backbone than seniority_final.
  • RQ3, employer-requirement / worker-usage divergence: unaffected by this audit, but any seniority-stratified divergence analysis must remember that seniority_final is conservative.
  • RQ4, mechanisms: not evaluated here.

The strongest alternative framing is not "junior roles disappeared." It is: explicit seniority labeling became more conservative while a substantial pool of junior-like postings remained hidden in unknown. That is a measurement story first, and only a labor-market story second.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text: seniority_final is the right column for coarse, high-confidence stratification and senior-role language work. It is not the right standalone measure for junior incidence. For RQ1, the YOE proxy should lead, with seniority_final used as a conservative lower bound and seniority_native as a diagnostic only.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: seniority_final is defensible as the primary seniority column for downstream exploration only if the downstream question is "what do explicit seniority signals say?" It is not defensible as the sole estimator of entry-level share. For any junior-share claim, lead with yoe_extracted <= 2, report seniority_final as a strict subset, and keep seniority_native arshkon-only as a diagnostic check.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: is_swe looks solid for the core SWE corpus, but the uncertainty is concentrated in a small title_lookup_llm tier and in boundary occupations. The classifier cleanly separates control occupations from SWE-adjacent ones, but it is least stable around QA/test, automation, firmware, architect, security, and some data/ML titles.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
I audited the LinkedIn English/date-ok frame only. The relevant rows are:

  • SWE: 59,972
  • SWE-adjacent: 22,279
  • control: 151,267

The SWE class itself is mostly rule-based:

Tier SWE rows Share of SWE regex 54,609 91.1% embedding_high 1,012 1.7% title_lookup_llm 4,351 7.3% unresolved 0 0.0%

The confidence-band request (swe_confidence 0.3-0.7) was empty in the LinkedIn SWE frame. The practical borderline set is therefore the title_lookup_llm tier.

I reviewed:

  1. A 50-row SWE borderline sample from the title_lookup_llm tier.
  2. A 50-row non-SWE sample where the title contains engineer, developer, or software.
  3. The top titles in is_swe_adjacent and is_control.
  4. A title-family matrix for boundary occupations.
  5. A dual-flag integrity check.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
The SWE borderline sample is not a random mess. It is mostly the expected edge zone: QA, test, automation, firmware, devsecops, AI/ML, architect, and senior developer titles. In the 50-row sample, 23 rows were clearly boundary-family titles and 27 were broader SWE-like titles.

That suggests the main uncertainty is not within the core SWE class. It is around whether roles like QA Engineer, Automation Engineer, Firmware Engineer, AI Engineer, Architect, or Software Engineering Manager belong on the SWE side or the SWE-adjacent side.

The non-SWE sample shows the complementary pattern. Clear non-SWE exclusions are mostly correct:

  • electrical, civil, mechanical, and structural engineer roles
  • financial / supply-chain / process engineering roles
  • non-software control occupations

The likely false negatives are concentrated in software-adjacent titles that are easy to over- or under-call:

  • RPA Developer, Appian Developer, NetSuite Developer, CRM Developer
  • Data Engineering / Manager Data Engineering
  • Forward Deployed Engineer
  • Staff Engineer, Payments Team
  • Software Design Quality Engineer
  • some Engineer titles with explicit software stacks or cloud context

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
The dual-flag check passes: is_swe + is_swe_adjacent + is_control > 1 is zero.

The core SWE tier is strong. The model is not over-relying on the uncertain tier, and regex dominates the final SWE corpus. The uncertainty share is also lower in 2026 than in 2024, with title_lookup_llm falling from about 9.7-10.9% of SWE rows in the 2024 LinkedIn sources to about 5.1-5.2% in scraped LinkedIn.

Estimated risk on the borderline slice:

  • false positives among borderline SWE rows: roughly 10-20%
  • false negatives among borderline non-SWE rows: roughly 10-20%

That is a coarse estimate from the 50-row review, not a formal classifier evaluation. The important part is where the errors live: boundary-family titles, not the core regex class.

docs.title: Surprises
docs.text:
Two boundary patterns stood out.

  1. data engineer, ml engineer, and devops/sre are mostly SWE in this corpus. They are not the main source of trouble.
  2. data scientist, security engineer, systems architect, and project engineer lean much more adjacent than SWE. QA/test engineer is mixed and appears to drift toward SWE in 2026.

The adjacent and control groups are semantically clean:

Adjacent top titles Control top titles quality engineer accountant data scientist registered nurse network engineer electrical engineer data analyst financial analyst architect / solutions architect mechanical engineer security engineer accounting manager

That separation is useful. It means the taxonomy is not just a thin wrapper around generic "engineer" language.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text:
• RQ1, employer-side restructuring: unaffected directly by this audit, but any seniority-stratified claim that leans on the SWE sample inherits the boundary-zone caveat.
  • RQ2, task and requirement migration: the boundary classes matter here because QA, test, automation, and DevOps are exactly the roles where requirement language can blur with adjacent work.
  • RQ3, employer-requirement / worker-usage divergence: unaffected directly.
  • RQ4, mechanisms: unaffected.

The strongest alternative framing is not that the classifier is broken. It is that the labor market itself has a wide technical boundary zone. The hardest roles to classify are the ones the market already treats as hybrids.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: is_swe is good enough for downstream exploration, but the title_lookup_llm tier should be treated as the main uncertainty bucket. Any later result that hinges on the SWE boundary should be re-run with that tier excluded or at least reported separately. Boundary-sensitive results should not be read as if SWE, SWE-adjacent, and control are equally crisp categories.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: The three LinkedIn source datasets are not interchangeable, but the mismatch is uneven. Description length clearly shifts upward from the 2024 Kaggle sources to scraped 2026, and that shift is larger than the within-2024 calibration gap. By contrast, company overlap, geographic composition, and title vocabulary are already highly source-dependent in 2024, so they do not provide a clean temporal signal. Seniority is the most fragile axis: seniority_final is much more conservative than the YOE proxy in every source, and the 2024 source baseline is noisy enough that junior-share claims need the label-independent check.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
• Current SWE counts on the default filtered frame are kaggle_arshkon 4,691, kaggle_asaniczka 18,129, and scraped 37,152 on LinkedIn; the scraped Indeed sensitivity sample adds 10,464 SWE rows.
  • Description length is higher in scraped 2026 than in either 2024 Kaggle source. Mean length is 3,306 chars for arshkon, 3,881 for asaniczka, and 4,936 for scraped LinkedIn. The cross-period length effect is about 2.6x the within-2024 effect.
  • Company, state, and title overlap are all low across sources, but the 2024 baseline is already low. For example, title Jaccard similarity is only 0.048 between arshkon and asaniczka and 0.027 between arshkon and scraped.
  • Seniority distributions are not stable enough to treat seniority_final as the only junior proxy. Overall entry share under seniority_final is 3.7% in arshkon, 0.9% in asaniczka, and 2.8% in scraped LinkedIn, while the YOE proxy (yoe_extracted <= 2) is 9.3%, 7.3%, and 11.6% respectively.
  • Indeed is a useful sanity check, not a contradiction. On scraped Indeed, entry share is 3.1% under seniority_final and 12.7% under the YOE proxy, which is close to the LinkedIn pattern rather than a platform-specific anomaly.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
• The seniority story is not “same direction, different magnitude.” In seniority_final, the cross-period seniority distribution change from arshkon to scraped is smaller than the arshkon-vs-asaniczka baseline, which means the 2024 instrument noise floor is already larger than the apparent 2024-to-2026 shift.
  • The title-level native label mix is unstable for generic titles. developer has the largest native-distribution gap in the shared-title sample, with the dominant native label shifting from entry in arshkon to mid-senior in scraped, even though the title’s YOE profile shift is much smaller than the label shift.
  • Industry labels are semantically uneven across sources. Arshkon’s top categories are broad labels like IT Services and IT Consulting and Software Development; scraped contains more compound or concatenated labels and a wider label inventory, so industry comparisons are descriptive only.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
• Description length: strong evidence. Large sample, large effect, survives aggregator exclusion. The cross-period Cohen’s d is -0.70, versus -0.28 within 2024.
  • Company overlap: moderate evidence for source mismatch, but not a temporal story. Jaccard distances are roughly 0.86-0.89 in all pairings, and the 2024 baseline is essentially the same size as the 2024-to-2026 gap.
  • Geography: moderate evidence of source composition differences, but not a useful temporal marker. Cramer’s V is 0.32-0.34 across all pairs, with almost no movement between within-2024 and cross-period comparisons.
  • Seniority: weak-to-moderate evidence for temporal change because the within-2024 baseline is much larger than the 2024-to-2026 comparison. seniority_final is also conservative relative to the YOE proxy, which makes it risky to lead with junior-share claims alone.
  • Title vocabulary: strong evidence that the three datasets are lexically different. Title Jaccard similarity is under 0.05 in every pair, and aggregator exclusion barely changes that fact.
  • Industry: weak-to-moderate evidence because asaniczka has no industry data and the raw industry strings are not semantically aligned across the other two sources.

docs.title: Calibration and comparability
docs.text:
The calibration table says that only description length is clearly above the 2024 instrument baseline. The ratio of cross-period to within-2024 effect size is about 2.56 for description length, but is about 1.00 for company overlap and state composition, 1.02 for title vocabulary, and only 0.29 for seniority. That means:

  1. Some 2026 changes are real on the text side.
  2. Many categorical differences are already present in 2024.
  3. Seniority needs the YOE proxy before it can support a junior-rung narrative.

docs.title: Platform labeling stability
docs.text:
The title-stability tables show that shared titles do not keep the same native seniority mix across periods. The clearest unstable titles are developer, systems engineer, quality assurance engineer, java developer, full stack developer, and test engineer. In the summary table, developer has the largest native seniority distance, and the dominant native label flips from entry in arshkon to mid-senior in scraped.

The YOE cell tables are more discriminating than the raw native-label tables. For several shared title/seniority cells, the YOE proxy moves less than the native label mix, which suggests a combination of relabeling and genuine composition shift. The largest cell shifts are in shared title cells like system engineer-entry, embedded software engineer-entry, software developer-entry, and site reliability engineer-entry.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion barely changes the qualitative story:

  • Description length still has the same direction and a similar large effect.
  • Company/title/state overlaps remain essentially unchanged.
  • Seniority differences remain smaller than the 2024 baseline either way.

That is useful because it means the core comparability warning is not an aggregator artifact.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• description_core_llm coverage is excellent in arshkon, still usable in asaniczka, and thin in scraped LinkedIn. For SWE rows, labeled coverage is 99.7% in arshkon, 85.9% in asaniczka, and 16.1% in scraped LinkedIn; Indeed has no LLM text coverage.
  • company_industry is unavailable in asaniczka and semantically uneven in the other sources, so it should not be treated as a strict categorical baseline.
  • seniority_final is conservative relative to the YOE proxy in every source, so it is useful as the primary seniority column only if it is always paired with the YOE floor check.
  • Multi-location postings are excluded from state rollups, and unresolved states are carried as an explicit missing category.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• Use seniority_final as the primary seniority column only alongside the YOE proxy, not instead of it.
  • Treat text analyses on scraped 2026 as coverage-restricted unless they are based on the raw description and clearly framed as binary presence checks.
  • Treat industry as descriptive context, not a precise cross-source control.
  • Read the title-stability tables before assuming that a title means the same thing in 2024 and 2026.
  • When a later finding is small, compare it to the within-2024 calibration first. If it is not larger than the baseline instrument noise, do not over-interpret it.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: Company concentration is not a side issue in this dataset. On the LinkedIn SWE frame, the top 50 employers account for 20.9% of arshkon postings, 30.6% of asaniczka postings, and 27.2% of scraped postings; those shares remain 18.3%, 23.5%, and 25.6% after aggregators are removed. Entry-level postings are even more concentrated: most firms with at least five SWE postings never post an entry role at all, and the companies that do post entry roles are a narrow subset. The overlap-panel decomposition shows that the 2024-to-2026 change is partly within-company, but company composition still matters a lot, especially for the YOE-based junior proxy.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
• Concentration is materially high in every source. The Gini coefficient is 0.43 in arshkon, 0.64 in asaniczka, and 0.69 in scraped. The top company share is 1.1% in arshkon, 5.0% in asaniczka, and 4.0% in scraped.
  • Residual exact duplicate templates exist but are limited. Only 14 arshkon companies, 42 asaniczka companies, and 112 scraped companies have more postings than distinct description hashes. Among companies with at least five SWE postings, that is 7, 27, and 94 companies respectively.
  • Entry posting is extremely employer-skewed. Under seniority_final, 79.3% of arshkon companies with at least five SWE postings have zero entry postings, 90.8% in asaniczka, and 82.0% in scraped. Under the YOE proxy, those zero-entry shares are still 56.7%, 58.1%, and 52.8%.
  • seniority_final and the YOE proxy do not identify the same specialist employers. seniority_final only surfaces a tiny set of clear entry specialists, while the YOE proxy surfaces a much broader set that includes Microsoft, Leidos, Lowe's, Medtronic, Wells Fargo, Google, Walmart, Qualcomm, and SpaceX.
  • The 2026 source is mostly made of new firms. Of the scraped LinkedIn companies, 6,527 are new relative to arshkon, versus 983 returning companies. Those new entrants are slightly more entry-heavy and a bit longer on average than the returning firms.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
• The strongest junior concentration signal depends on the seniority instrument. seniority_final shows a slight decline in entry share from 2024 to 2026, while the YOE proxy shows a sizable increase. That is not a cosmetic difference; it changes the direction of the apparent story.
  • The overlap-panel decomposition says the junior story is largely about company composition when the YOE proxy is used. The within-company component is near zero for entry_yoe, while the between-company component explains almost all of the change.
  • The residual duplicate-template problem is real but much smaller than the older pipeline behavior that motivated this task. The multi-location fix seems to have removed the broad, obvious duplication issue; what remains is a concentrated set of staffing or templated employers.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
• Concentration metrics: strong evidence. Large samples, no modeling assumptions, and the pattern survives aggregator exclusion.
  • Duplicate-template audit: strong as a diagnostic. The residual duplicates are not huge in count, but they are structurally important because they can dominate naïve corpus-level metrics.
  • Entry concentration: strong evidence that entry roles are employer-specific, not market-wide. The zero-entry shares among firms with at least five SWE postings are too large to ignore.
  • Decomposition: moderate evidence. It is panel-restricted to firms present in both arshkon and scraped LinkedIn with at least five SWE postings, and the text proxies are coarse, but the within-versus-between split is still informative.
  • New entrants: strong evidence of source-composition shift. The 2026 source is dominated by firms that are absent from the 2024 benchmark.

docs.title: Entry concentration
docs.text:
The entry-level pool is concentrated enough that aggregate entry-share plots are misleading if read as “the market.” The company-level stats show a median company entry share of zero under seniority_final in every source, and the YOE proxy only raises the median to zero while pushing the upper tail up sharply.

The clearest entry specialists under seniority_final are:

  • arshkon: SynergisticIT
  • scraped: SynergisticIT, WayUp, Emonics LLC, Tech Consulting, CGI Technologies and Solutions, SkillStorm, Bose Corporation

Under the YOE proxy, the specialist set is broader and more plausible as a junior-market lens:

  • arshkon: Microsoft, Leidos, Lowe's Companies, Inc., Cogent Communications, Tech Consulting, Qualcomm, Macquarie Group
  • asaniczka: Medtronic, Wells Fargo, Ball Aerospace, Anyscale, RoviSys, ADP, Bell Flight, The Reynolds and Reynolds Company, Alcon, Lark
  • scraped: Google, Walmart, Qualcomm, SpaceX, Visa, VIZIO, AaraTechnologies Inc, Latitude AI, Super Micro Computer Spain, S.L., Snap Inc.

That gap is one of the most important measurement findings in the exploration.

docs.title: Decomposition
docs.text:
The decomposition table is the cleanest way to separate firm behavior from firm composition.

  • entry_final: 2024 = 3.16%, 2026 = 2.64%, change = -0.52 pp. Within-company change is -0.87 pp and between-company change is +0.35 pp, so the within-company decline is partially offset by composition.
  • entry_yoe: 2024 = 9.10%, 2026 = 15.88%, change = +6.78 pp. The within-company component is essentially zero, while the between-company component is +6.80 pp. That means the YOE-based junior rise is mostly a composition story.
  • ai_mention: 2024 = 13.8%, 2026 = 53.1%, change = +39.3 pp. About 71% of that change is within-company, so the AI-language rise is not just new firms entering the panel.
  • description_length: 2024 = 3,506 chars, 2026 = 5,277 chars, change = +1,771 chars. Roughly 54% of that change is within-company and 46% is between-company.
  • tech_count: 2024 = 5.25, 2026 = 6.33, change = +1.08. The within-company component is positive, while the between-company component is slightly negative, so company mix dampens the tech-count rise.

The key interpretive point is that junior hiring is not one thing. The seniority_final version and the YOE proxy point in different directions, and the YOE proxy is much more composition-driven.

docs.title: Prediction table
docs.text:
The concentration prediction table should be treated as a routing guide for Wave 2 and Wave 3:

  • entry_share: high concentration risk
  • ai_mention_rate: moderate-high concentration risk
  • description_length: moderate concentration risk
  • term_frequencies: high concentration risk
  • topic_models: high concentration risk
  • co_occurrence_networks: high concentration risk

The practical default is simple: cap by company, deduplicate exact templates, and report company-weighted versions whenever a corpus-level metric is used.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion matters, but it does not change the qualitative ranking.

  • Top-50 share falls from 20.9% to 18.3% in arshkon, 30.6% to 23.5% in asaniczka, and 27.2% to 25.6% in scraped.
  • The concentration picture stays steep even after the exclusion.
  • The entry concentration story is still extremely employer-skewed after the exclusion.

That means aggregators amplify the concentration problem, but they are not the whole problem.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• The company counts are based on known canonical company names. Missing company names are rare in arshkon and essentially absent elsewhere, but they are still tracked separately in the concentration table.
  • The duplicate-template audit is about exact description hashes, so it undercounts near-duplicate boilerplate collisions. It is a diagnostic, not a complete deduplication audit.
  • The decomposition uses companies with at least five SWE postings in both arshkon and scraped LinkedIn. It is intentionally a panel of surviving employers, not the whole market.
  • AI mention and tech-count decomposition pieces are coarse raw-text proxies. They are good enough to show concentration sensitivity, but not yet a substitute for the later cleaned-text pipeline.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• Treat any corpus-level term-frequency or topic result as concentration-sensitive by default.
  • Use company caps and deduplication before building term networks or topic models.
  • Read the entry-specialist tables before making claims about “junior hiring”; the answer depends on the seniority instrument.
  • When a later result looks like a market-wide shift, check whether it is actually new-company composition or a narrow group of repeated employers.
  • Use the prediction table as the first stop before Wave 2 or Wave 3 text work.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: Most of the planned cross-period analyses are statistically feasible, but not all are equally strong. The clear binding constraint is the entry-level comparison: 175 arshkon entry postings versus 1,042 scraped entry postings gives a standardized binary MDE of 0.229, which I would treat as marginal. By contrast, mid-senior, senior, all-SWE, and pooled-2024 comparisons are well-powered by the same 80% / 0.05 standard. The geographic benchmark is also reassuring: our state-level SWE distribution is very close to BLS software-developer employment geography, with Pearson r ≈ 0.97 in every slice I could benchmark locally.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
• The current default-filter LinkedIn corpus contains 1,186,281 rows and 59,972 SWE rows. The SWE sample is split into 4,691 arshkon, 18,129 asaniczka, and 37,152 scraped SWE postings.
  • Entry-level work is the tightest sample. 175 arshkon entry postings versus 1,042 scraped entry postings yields MDE_binary = 0.229 and MDE_continuous = 0.229, so entry-share claims are feasible but not comfortable.
  • Everything above entry-level is fine for first-pass work. Mid-senior, senior, all SWE, and pooled 2024 all fall in the well-powered bucket, with standardized MDEs between 0.066 and 0.024.
  • Metro analysis is feasible, but strict metro panels are narrow. After excluding multi-location postings, the arshkon 2024-04 slice has 18 metros with at least 50 SWE postings and 8 with at least 100. The combined scraped 2026-03+04 slice has 18 metros that clear 50 in both periods and only 8 that clear 100 in both.
  • The overlap panel for company fixed-effects work is usable. There are 237 companies with at least 3 SWE postings in both arshkon and scraped, and 122 companies that clear 5 in both. Using pooled 2024 instead of arshkon alone expands that to 572 companies at >=3 and 323 at >=5.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
• The geographic fit is stronger than I expected. Even with incomplete state coverage in our own postings frame, the BLS comparison still lands around r = 0.97 for arshkon 2024-04, scraped 2026-03, and scraped 2026-04.
  • The sample-size bottleneck is narrow and specific. The rest of the planned comparison families are not merely “possible”; they are comfortably powered under standard assumptions.
  • The company-overlap panel is smaller than the raw source totals might suggest, but still large enough to support fixed-effects or within-company decomposition work. The >=5 threshold is the real pressure point, not >=3.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
Analysis n_group1 n_group2 MDE_binary MDE_continuous Verdict Entry arshkon vs scraped 175 1,042 0.229 0.229 marginal Mid-senior arshkon vs scraped 2,064 15,534 0.066 0.066 well-powered Senior arshkon vs scraped 2,077 15,799 0.065 0.065 well-powered All SWE arshkon vs scraped 4,691 37,152 0.043 0.043 well-powered Pooled 2024 vs scraped 22,820 37,152 0.024 0.024 well-powered

I am treating the MDEs as standardized effect sizes (Cohen's h for binary and Cohen's d for continuous). That avoids arbitrary baseline assumptions and is the right level of abstraction for a feasibility screen.

docs.title: External benchmark context
docs.text:
The state-level BLS comparison is strong enough to use as context, not as causal validation. The current 2024 OEWS state table I could scrape cleanly surfaces 15-1252 Software Developers directly; the combined 15-1256 naming in BLS guidance is a nomenclature caveat rather than a separate current state row in the table I finished. On the 51-state BLS benchmark, the overlap with our postings frame is high enough to be useful, and the Pearson correlations are consistently around 0.97:

Period States matched Pearson r arshkon 2024-04 47 0.9718 scraped 2026-03 31 0.9747 scraped 2026-04 29 0.9729

That tells me the sample is not geographically bizarre. It does not validate any substantive trend, but it does make state-level comparisons less fragile than I expected.

The industry labels in our own sample are also unsurprising in broad outline. The largest categories are IT Services and IT Consulting (1,202), Software Development (611), Financial Services (391), Staffing and Recruiting (271), and Information Services (150). That is directionally consistent with the BLS story that software-developer employment is concentrated in software-heavy and systems-integration-heavy industries, but the company-industry field is too noisy and uneven to treat as a formal benchmark.

docs.title: Benchmark caveats
docs.text:
• I stopped the FRED/JOLTS download before completion, so there is no hiring-cycle figure in this run. That is an external-benchmark gap, not a data problem.
  • Metro counts exclude multi-location postings, as required, and the excluded SWE count in the current default-filter frame is 3,901.
  • The BLS state benchmark is complete enough for context, but it should not be over-read as a test of causality or representativeness beyond geography.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text: The data can support a real restructuring story, but only if the analysis is disciplined about sample size. The broad market-level comparisons are safe; the entry-level story is the one that needs the most care. The geography is reassuringly aligned with the BLS benchmark, which helps the paper’s external validity, but the strong result here is still the feasibility map: most planned analyses are usable, and the entry-level slice is the only one that should be treated as fragile.

docs.title: Direction for next wave
docs.text:
• Treat entry-level claims as provisional and always pair them with the label-independent YOE proxy.
  • Proceed with seniority, AI-language, and company-overlap analyses; those are well-powered.
  • Use metro work with a >=50 exploratory threshold and reserve >=100 for the strictest cross-period comparisons.
  • Use >=3 company overlap as the primary panel definition and >=5 as the robustness cut.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: If the paper stops looking at the feasibility layer and moves into substantive findings, it should not lead with geography or benchmark validation. The best role for T07 is as a guardrail: it says which later analyses are credible and which ones are thin. The analysis phase should lead with well-powered cross-period and within-company restructuring results, and keep entry-level claims carefully qualified.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: The SWE LinkedIn frame is structurally more volatile than the initial design suggested. The most important pattern is not a clean junior collapse; it is a split between a conservative explicit-entry label and a broader YOE proxy. seniority_final = entry stays tiny and increasingly conservative in 2026, while yoe_extracted <= 2 rises from 9.3% in arshkon to 11.7% in scraped 2026-03 and 11.5% in scraped 2026-04. That divergence survives the known-seniority denominator and points to measurement conservatism, not just a change in the labor market. On the text side, the dominant change is still description expansion and scope/AI language growth, and those changes are much larger than the 2024 baseline calibration noise.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
• The LinkedIn SWE frame is 59,972 rows, with 9,470 aggregator rows. The current wave is well-powered for descriptive profiling, but not for pooled label claims that ignore source-specific coverage.
  • seniority_final is a strict lower-bound junior label. Explicit-entry share is 3.7% in arshkon, 3.4% in scraped 2026-03, and 2.2% in scraped 2026-04, but the YOE proxy remains above 11% in both scraped periods.
  • The direction mismatch between seniority_final and the YOE proxy is not a denominator trick. Even among rows with known seniority, entry share falls from 7.7% in arshkon to 4.9% in scraped 2026-04, while YOE<=2 rises from 15.0% to 17.0%. That is a real measurement asymmetry.
  • On the mid-senior calibration subset, mean description length rises from 3,719.7 chars in arshkon to 5,486.2 in scraped 2026, and the effect size is 0.785 d versus 0.245 d within 2024. That is the cleanest large change in the profiling wave.
  • Ranked by absolute cross-period effect, the next largest mid-senior shifts are boilerplate share, AI-any/tool share, scope-term share, CI/CD share, and llm_share. In the top-tech stack, C++ falls the most, while Go, Kubernetes, AWS, GCP, and TypeScript rise. Those are weaker than the text-level shifts, but they are the clearest technology-stack movements in this wave.
  • Text-heavy signals are concentrated in a subset of employers and in the cleaned-text coverage frame. Only 16.1% of scraped SWE rows have LLM-cleaned text, so any 2026 text claim needs coverage reporting and a sensitivity check.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
• seniority_native = 'entry' is not a stable baseline in arshkon. Those rows average 4.18 YOE with median 3.0, so the native label cannot be used as a cross-period truth source.
  • The geography frame is thinner than expected. Metro missingness runs from 24.5% in scraped 2026-04 to 40.8% in arshkon, and the number of distinct metro areas collapses to 27 in scraped 2026, versus 74 in arshkon and 87 in asaniczka.
  • Industry labels are semantically uneven. Arshkon has 163 distinct industry strings; scraped 2026 has 1,000+ distinct labels in the same frame, which is a label-formatting issue as much as a market signal.
  • Company size is almost entirely an arshkon-only diagnostic. That makes it useful for within-source structure, but not as a cross-period explanatory variable.

docs.title: Strong
docs.text:
• Description length expansion. Large sample, same direction across source pairings, and the cross-period shift is larger than the within-2024 calibration gap.
  • Text coverage asymmetry. The LLM-cleaned text share is 99.7% in arshkon, 85.9% in asaniczka, and 16.1% in scraped LinkedIn.
  • Metro and industry concentration. The geography and industry slices show sharp source-dependent concentration patterns and more missingness than a naive panel interpretation would suggest.

docs.title: Moderate
docs.text:
• The YOE proxy itself. It is broader than seniority_final, but it still depends on parsed YOE availability and inherits source coverage differences.
  • Company-size stratification. Within arshkon, larger employers are clearly more expansive and slightly more junior by YOE, but this cannot be generalized to scraped or asaniczka.
  • The text-growth calibration table. The main signal is robust, and the company-capped sensitivity keeps the same sign on the core text metrics, but some term-level changes are still sensitive to employer concentration.

docs.title: Weak or artifact-prone
docs.text:
• seniority_3level for junior work. It collapses almost all known rows into senior, so it is not a useful junior estimator.
  • Cross-source industry comparisons. The labels are not semantically aligned enough to support strong cross-period interpretation.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text:
• RQ1, employer-side restructuring: partially supported, but the simple junior-decline framing is not. The stronger result is conservative explicit-entry labeling plus a broader junior-like YOE base.
  • RQ2, task and requirement migration: supported in the text domain, especially for description expansion, scope language, and AI language, but the text claims must be framed with coverage limits.
  • RQ3, employer-requirement / worker-usage divergence: still open. The present wave adds no direct worker-side benchmark, only a stronger case that employer-side requirements are changing.
  • RQ4, mechanisms: not tested directly here, but the measurement asymmetry strengthens the case for a qualitative mechanism check.

Two alternative framings are still live. One is an expansion framing: the market is not simply eliminating juniors; it is expanding the explicit requirement surface and writing fewer postings as clean entry labels. The other is a conservatism framing: the 2026 pipeline is less willing to assign entry labels, so the apparent junior decline is partly a label system artifact. The data currently support the second more strongly than the first, but the YOE rise means there is still a real labor-market signal underneath the artifact.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text: The best current narrative is not "junior roles vanished." It is: the observable junior rung became more conservative and less explicit, while broader junior-like YOE requirements remained common and may even have risen slightly. In parallel, postings got longer, more scope-heavy, and more AI-heavy. That is a stronger and more defensible story than a pure junior-collapse claim.

docs.title: Research question evolution
docs.text:
RQ1 should be rewritten to ask how explicit seniority labeling, YOE floors, and employer composition jointly changed the observable junior rung. That is more precise than claiming a single junior-share trend.

RQ2 should keep the task/requirement migration framing, but the highest-value measure is now the expansion of requirement breadth and scope language, not a single junior-senior migration count.

RQ3 remains provisional until the worker-side benchmark is compared explicitly.

RQ4 becomes more important, not less, because the data now show that label conservatism, company concentration, and template inflation all matter.

docs.title: Gaps and weaknesses
docs.text:
• Cleaned-text coverage is thin in scraped 2026.
  • company_industry and metro_area are useful profiling variables, but not clean causal controls.
  • The archetype decomposition requested in T08 could not be run because ../../../assets/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet is not present yet.
  • The current wave does not resolve whether 2026 text growth is mostly requirements, boilerplate, or recruiter template inflation; it only shows that the growth is real.

docs.title: Direction for next wave
docs.text: The next wave should dig into the text-content split: requirements versus boilerplate versus credential language, and it should cap employer concentration by default. The strongest follow-up is to test whether the 2026 text expansion is mostly substantive or mostly template inflation, then connect that to archetype structure once the domain labels arrive.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: If we stopped here, the paper should not lead with junior decline. It should lead with posting-language expansion plus a measurement warning: explicit seniority labels became more conservative while the YOE proxy and text measures still show a meaningful shift. That supports an empirical restructuring paper, but not a simplistic junior-elimination one.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: The SWE posting space is organized much more by tech/domain bundles than by seniority or period, and the first representation that makes that visible is NMF k=15, not BERTopic. The selected archetypes split the market into a domain-first map: requirements/compliance, backend/API and backend/data-platform, frontend/web and frontend/angular, DevOps/infra/tooling, embedded/firmware/systems, data engineering/ETL, mobile/iOS, AI/LLM workflows, and a generic workflow/admin layer. The clearest 2026 expansion is the AI/LLM archetype, which is 91.5% 2026 in the full-corpus labels.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
I built a balanced primary sample of 5,400 SWE LinkedIn rows, 2,700 from 2024 and 2,700 from 2026. The 2024 side uses arshkon only, because it has the best text quality and entry coverage; the 2026 side uses scraped LinkedIn only. The primary sample is all text_source = 'llm'. Company caps and exact company/text deduplication were applied before sampling. The shared embeddings cover 26,219 llm rows overall, but the primary sample uses only the llm subset.

I ran BERTopic at min_topic_size = 20, 30, 50 and NMF at k = 5, 8, 12, 15. Raw-text sensitivity used a separate raw-fallback sample of 4,592 rows. The downstream label artifact, swe_archetype_labels.parquet, is built from NMF k = 15.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
• BERTopic is not the best downstream representation here. It returns only 3-6 topics, mostly a boilerplate requirements cluster, an AI/LLM cluster, and a mobile cluster. Noise is low, but the semantic resolution is too coarse for the rest of the exploration.
  • NMF is the useful representation, but not at the smallest k. k = 5 and k = 8 maximize coherence/stability by collapsing the market into too few buckets. k = 15 is the first setting that cleanly separates backend, data, infra, embedded, AI, frontend, mobile, and requirement-related work.
  • The strongest structural signal is tech domain. On the full corpus, tech_domain has the highest NMI with the selected archetypes (0.123), while seniority (0.003), period (0.028), source (0.030), text source (0.013), and aggregator status (0.007) are all much weaker.
  • The largest full-corpus archetypes are requirements/compliance (22.7%), generic workflow/admin (9.4%), embedded/firmware (8.4%), cross-functional/delivery (7.5%), DevOps/infra (6.8%), AI/LLM workflows (6.6%), backend/API (5.6%), DevOps/tooling (5.5%), frontend/web (5.2%), and backend/data-platform (5.6%).

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
• The market map is not seniority-first. seniority_3level barely moves the archetypes, and the production seniority column is even less aligned.
  • The AI/LLM archetype is real but not the whole story. It is one of several domain families, not a universal overlay on the entire sample.
  • Raw fallback does not destroy the domain map, but it makes boilerplate more visible. The raw sensitivity mostly preserves the big families while pushing more rows into compliance/legal and generic boilerplate topics.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
method config topics coherence stability noise read NMF k=5 5 0.608 1.000 0.0 too coarse for downstream use NMF k=8 8 0.544 0.981 0.0 still too coarse NMF k=12 12 0.548 0.768 0.0 better, but still leaves domain splits under-resolved NMF k=15 15 0.562 0.785 0.0 selected downstream labeler BERTopic min_topic_size=20 6 0.488 0.511 0.0 coarse macro-topics BERTopic min_topic_size=30 6 0.467 0.491 1.6% coarse macro-topics BERTopic min_topic_size=50 3 0.410 0.506 0.8% too collapsed to use downstream

The key tradeoff is obvious in the table: coherence/stability alone would select k = 5, but that is not a credible archetype map. k = 15 is the best compromise between stability and publishable granularity.

docs.title: Methods comparison
docs.text:
BERTopic and NMF agree on a small set of robust themes. The strong overlaps are AI/LLM workflows and Mobile/iOS; requirements/compliance also overlaps, but mostly as a generic boilerplate bucket. Everything else that matters downstream is NMF-specific because BERTopic collapses backend, data, infra, and embedded into much broader blobs.

The practical recommendation is to use BERTopic only as a coarse discovery check and to use the NMF k = 15 labels for the rest of the exploration. That is what the full-corpus label artifact now does.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text: The raw-fallback sensitivity preserves the same broad families, but it is noisier and more boilerplate-heavy. The strongest raw alignment is still requirements/compliance, AI/LLM workflows, and frontend/web. Raw text makes the compliance/legal bucket more visible and weakens some backend/infra boundaries, which is exactly why the primary analysis stays on text_source = 'llm'.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• The primary sample is intentionally not proportional: it over-represents junior and mid rows relative to the corpus so those strata are visible in an unsupervised model.
  • company_size is only meaningfully available on arshkon, so company-size alignment is a weak, arshkon-heavy diagnostic.
  • The archetypes are posting-form bundles as much as substantive role families. Boilerplate, compliance, and legal language remain a real part of the market’s observable structure.

docs.title: Current paper position
docs.text: T09 shifts the framing away from a pure seniority story and toward domain recomposition. The most credible headline is that SWE postings are now organized around tech/domain archetypes, with AI/LLM as the clearest 2026 growth bundle, while seniority and source instrument explain very little of the latent structure.

docs.title: Downstream action items
docs.text:
1. Use swe_archetype_labels.parquet as the domain stratifier for T11, T16, T20, and T28.
  2. Treat NMF k = 15 as the downstream archetype representation.
  3. Use BERTopic only as a coarse comparator and noise/outlier check.
  4. When a later result looks like a market-wide change, check whether it is really domain recomposition, especially into AI/LLM.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: The title landscape is lexically noisy, but not clearly more fragmented in 2026. Unique titles per 1,000 SWE postings are basically flat across the historical baseline and the scraped window: arshkon is 460.9, asaniczka is 417.9, and scraped is 457.3. After removing aggregators, the rate is still close: 430.0 for arshkon versus 400.4 for scraped. The change that does show up cleanly is semantic. Broad AI-related title vocabulary rises sharply, and the new 2026 titles are concentrated in AI/ML, general SWE, embedded/systems, full stack, and data/analytics.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
I compared LinkedIn SWE titles only, using arshkon 2024 as the historical baseline and pooled scraped 2026 as the current window. I also used asaniczka as the within-2024 calibration source. The analysis covers:

  • title overlap and Jaccard similarity across sources
  • unique titles per 1,000 SWE postings, with and without aggregators
  • broad AI-related title share and explicit seniority-marker share
  • new versus disappeared title lists and theme summaries
  • seniority distribution shifts for the 10 most common titles shared by arshkon and scraped
  • TF-IDF cosine similarity between aggregate descriptions for those shared titles, using the cleaned-text llm subset only

The title-content similarity analysis is text-sensitive, so it uses the cleaned llm subset from ../../../assets/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet. That matters because some historically common titles, especially dotnet developer, do not have enough labeled 2026 text to support a clean comparison.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
• Exact title overlap is low, but it was already low in 2024. The title-set Jaccard is 0.048 for arshkon vs asaniczka and 0.027 for arshkon vs scraped. That means lexical mismatch is real, but it is not a uniquely 2026 phenomenon.
  • The title concentration story is weak. The cross-period change in unique titles per 1,000 is not clearly above the 2024 baseline noise floor. If anything, the no-aggregator view is slightly more standardized in 2026 than in arshkon.
  • AI-related title language is the clearest title-level shift. Broad AI/ML/data/agent title share rises from 12.4% in arshkon to 22.7% in scraped. The cleaner ai_domain share rises only modestly, from 8.9% to 9.8%, which suggests the big change is broad AI/ML/data naming rather than only pure AI-tool titles.
  • New titles are not just AI titles. They are led by General SWE titles, then AI/ML, Embedded/Systems, Full Stack, and Data/Analytics. The disappearing set is mostly legacy stack-specific variants, not a collapse of canonical software titles.
  • Same titles are usually still the same jobs, but not perfectly. Average TF-IDF cosine similarity for the top shared titles is 0.715 for arshkon vs scraped, compared with 0.799 for arshkon vs asaniczka. The biggest drifts are in Java, Salesforce, test, and embedded/system titles; software engineer and data engineer stay comparatively stable.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
• The strongest title finding is not fragmentation. It is title re-anchoring toward AI/ML and adjacent role families. The title count rate barely moves, but the semantics of new titles do.
  • Some of the “new” titles are really formatting or modifier variants, such as software engineer [multiple positions available] or software engineer - level. That means title churn includes posting-format noise, not only occupational change.
  • The historical baseline already contains a lot of source-specific lexical drift. That makes a pure “2026 is different” story too simple.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
• Title concentration: weak. The normalized unique-title rate is nearly flat, and the signal-to-noise ratio versus the 2024 baseline is only 0.08 for unique titles per 1,000. This is not a good headline result.
  • Broad AI-related titles: strong. The signal-to-noise ratio is 2.77, so the rise is materially above within-2024 variation.
  • Explicit seniority markers in titles: weak. These are rare to begin with, and the share is small in every source.
  • Title-content alignment: moderate. The same titles are mostly semantically stable, but the cross-period similarity drop relative to the 2024 baseline is real enough to report. This is strongest for a few title families rather than the whole title space.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
• Aggregator exclusion does not change the core title conclusion.
  • The overlap story is stable under the 2024 calibration check: the 2024 baseline already has low overlap and lexical variability.
  • The title-content similarity result is text-coverage sensitive. It is based on the cleaned llm subset, and dotnet developer has no 2026 labeled text, so it drops out of the similarity comparison.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text: The original “title taxonomy is fragmenting” story is not the best framing. The data support a more precise one: the title layer is re-anchoring toward AI/ML and specialized platform / systems families, while canonical SWE titles remain dominant and concentration barely moves. That is a language shift, not a wholesale occupation split.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text: The title layer looks like a lexicon under pressure from AI and specialization, but not a title space that has blown apart. The real novelty is the rise of AI/ML-adjacent naming and the modest semantic drift inside common titles. The market is still using familiar title scaffolds, just with more AI, platform, and infrastructure labeling attached.

docs.title: Research question evolution
docs.text:
RQ1 should not be read as just “junior share changed.” Titles suggest an additional sub-question: how did the naming layer itself re-encode SWE work, especially AI/ML and platform specialization?

Proposed addition:

RQ1b: How did the lexical title layer re-encode SWE roles between 2024 and 2026, especially around AI/ML, platform, embedded/systems, and infrastructure titles?

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• The title space is sparse enough that exact overlap is informative but not decisive on its own.
  • Some “new” and “disappeared” titles are formatting variants, not real occupational categories.
  • Title-content alignment is coverage-limited because some common titles have thin llm text in 2026.

docs.title: Direction for next wave
docs.text: The title results point away from a pure fragmentation narrative and toward semantic re-labeling. The next wave should focus on whether those title changes are matched by actual requirement changes in the text, or whether they are mostly labels on top of similar work.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: Title evolution should support the paper, not lead it. The best use of T10 is as evidence that the title layer is drifting toward AI and specialization while leaving the canonical SWE core intact. The main story still needs the requirement-complexity and boilerplate analyses to separate real demand change from naming drift.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: The complexity story is real, but it is not “more tech” in a simple sense. The robust change is that 2026 postings ask for more requirement categories at once: AI, scope, YOE, soft skills, and technology, bundled together more often. On the primary company-capped llm subset, requirement_breadth and credential_stack_depth both rise from 2024 to 2026, while tech_count only moves modestly and is not clearly above the 2024 calibration baseline. Scope density and AI mentions are the clearest increases. Explicit management language is much less clean than the rest of the signal.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
I built a posting-level complexity extractor on top of the shared cleaned-text artifact and shared technology matrix. The primary specification is:

  • LinkedIn SWE only
  • text_source = 'llm'
  • company cap of 25 postings per company per source-period
  • all rows included for the primary estimate
  • explicit reporting of no-cap, no-aggregator, and raw-text sensitivities

The extractor counts:

  • tech_count from the shared technology matrix
  • soft-skill terms
  • organizational-scope terms
  • strong management terms
  • broad management terms
  • AI-specific requirements
  • education level, taking the highest degree mentioned
  • YOE presence from yoe_extracted

Then it materializes:

  • requirement_breadth: sum of distinct term hits across categories
  • credential_stack_depth: number of categories touched
  • tech_density and scope_density: per 1,000 characters

I report explicit-entry and YOE-proxy versions side by side, because the seniority work in Wave 1 showed that seniority_final is conservative for junior-like postings.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
• Complexity rose, but unevenly. In the primary llm-cleaned, company-capped sample, explicit-entry postings in arshkon move from 3.60 mean breadth / 1.82 stack depth to 4.24 / 2.12 in scraped 2026. The YOE-proxy junior slice is broader still: 4.67 / 2.50 in arshkon to 5.41 / 2.88 in scraped.
  • The clearest breadth change is scope, not tech. scope_density roughly doubles between arshkon and scraped in the junior slices, and scope_count is one of the metrics that clears the 2024 calibration baseline by a wide margin.
  • AI is a robust new layer. ai_count rises sharply and survives the within-2024 calibration check by a large margin. This is the most obvious non-boilerplate addition to the requirement stack.
  • tech_count does increase, but only modestly. Its 2024-to-2026 change is not clearly above the 2024 instrument baseline, so “more tech” is not the clean headline.
  • Education is sparse and not a lead driver. Degree mentions exist, but they do not explain the complexity rise.
  • Management language is noisy. The broad tier is dominated by generic team language, while the strong tier is cleaner but small. I would not lead with management as the main complexity result.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
• The explicit-entry slice is not representative of the broader junior-like population. The YOE proxy is consistently more complex than explicit seniority_final = entry, which is exactly the measurement gap Wave 1 warned about.
  • Raw text sensitivity matters, but it does not reverse the story. On scraped 2026, raw fallback raises breadth by about 6 percent and stack depth by about 4 to 6 percent, while densities fall because raw descriptions are longer. That is a magnitude problem, not a direction problem.
  • The most complex postings look real. The top 1 percent by breadth includes roles such as senior software, AI platform, machine-learning, and infrastructure-heavy postings. They do not read like pure template bloating.
  • I spot-checked 100 management matches, balanced across the four period bins, and the sample was dominated by the broad team trigger rather than the strong management tier. That is exactly why the broad tier should stay a sensitivity, not a headline.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
• Requirement breadth: strong. The 2024-vs-2026 signal-to-noise ratio is 5.4.
  • Credential stack depth: very strong. The signal-to-noise ratio is 22.5.
  • AI count: very strong. The signal-to-noise ratio is 6.6.
  • Scope count / scope density: very strong. This is the cleanest “scope inflation” style signal in the task.
  • Tech count: moderate at best. It moves, but not enough to lead the story on its own.
  • Management broad / management strong: weak as a period signal. Broad management is too generic; strong management is cleaner but sparse.
  • Education flag: weak. It does not carry the complexity story.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
• Company cap: breadth and stack depth are stable under capping, but density metrics are not. tech_density is especially sensitive to no-cap estimates, which is exactly what we expect with concentrated employers.
  • Aggregator exclusion: does not change the qualitative story.
  • Raw text fallback: the raw fallback in 2026 nudges breadth and stack upward a little, but the direction remains the same. 2024 raw coverage is too thin to treat as a meaningful baseline, so raw should stay a sensitivity rather than a second headline.
  • Seniority operationalization: explicit-entry results are materially lower than YOE-proxy results. That disagreement is substantive and should be reported, not smoothed over.
  • Within-2024 calibration: requirement_breadth, credential_stack_depth, scope_count, ai_count, text_len, and scope_density all clear the calibration bar. tech_count, education_flag, and management measures do not cleanly do so.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text: The original RQ2 framing is directionally right, but too narrow if it stays focused on “requirement migration” in the old sense. The stronger result is credential stacking: postings are bundling more requirement categories together, with AI and scope doing most of the work. Junior-scope inflation is real in the YOE proxy, but the more publishable claim is that junior-like postings are being asked to satisfy more kinds of constraints simultaneously.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text: The data support a “more categories at once” story. 2026 SWE postings are not simply longer or more technical; they are more multi-constraint, with AI, scope, and YOE requirements showing the clearest rise. The strongest version of the story is not “more tech,” but “more credential stacking around the same SWE core.”

docs.title: Research question evolution
docs.text:
RQ2 should be expanded. The cleanest addition is:

RQ2b: Did SWE postings in 2026 require more categories of capability simultaneously than in 2024, and which categories drove the increase?

The current evidence suggests that AI and scope should be central in that question, not optional side examples.

RQ1 should also be reframed slightly: the junior question is better posed in terms of explicit labels versus YOE-proxy junior-like postings, because the two are not measuring the same thing.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• The cleaned-text sample is thin in scraped 2026, so every text result should be read with coverage in mind.
  • The strong/broad management split is important because the broad tier is noisy. Generic team language is the dominant trigger.
  • The domain-stratified scope branch is deferred because the T09 archetype labels are not present in the shared artifacts.

docs.title: Direction for next wave
docs.text: The next wave should separate content from boilerplate more aggressively and, if possible, test whether the scope/AI stack survives section stripping. It should also de-emphasize broad management language and keep the YOE proxy attached to any junior claim.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: This task pushes the paper away from a pure “junior share fell” framing and toward a stronger restructuring story: SWE postings are becoming more multi-constraint, more AI-saturated, and more scope-heavy. That is a substantive result, not just a measurement artifact.
