docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
The cleaned full-text comparison is not a clean symmetric 2024-vs-2026 vocabulary shift. After company capping, the top 100 log-odds terms are still dominated by the historical side, especially stack and credential artifacts such as javascript, jquery, directory, j2ee, wordpress, html, laravel, jdbc, visualforce, and similar tokens. That is useful information, but it is also a warning sign: the cleaned surface is still carrying a lot of old-form noise.

The section-filtered comparison does not fully solve that problem because it is extremely imbalanced: only 72 arshkon documents have detected section text versus 3,359 scraped documents. That makes the section-filtered list informative as a direction check, but not a balanced test. The strongest 2026 signal actually shows up in the sensitivity checks and the topic model, not in the main full-text ranking.

In the raw same-row sensitivity, the 2026 side surfaces AI language that the cleaned full-text ranking obscures: maintain, AIML, AI powered, AI driven, LLMs, LLM, domains, and similar terms. The emergent-term tables make the same point more sharply. AI assisted, Claude, multi agent, langgraph, embeddings, AI enabled, LLM based, multimodal, and AI generated all rise sharply in 2026.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
The biggest surprise is how one-sided the main cleaned-text ranking remains even after a careful preprocessing pipeline. I expected a more balanced list with some obvious 2026 AI terms. Instead, the dominant signal is still old stack vocabulary and cleaning artifacts. That is not a reason to discard the analysis. It is a reason to treat full cleaned text as a noisy surface rather than the final estimator.

The second surprise is how revealing the raw same-row branch is. When the exact same rows are compared on raw descriptions, the AI language becomes much easier to see. That means the cleaning pipeline is not just removing junk; it is also suppressing some of the modern signal, probably because the modern signal is entangled with template text and repeated generic phrasing.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
The primary full-text comparison is moderate-to-strong evidence for broad language divergence, but weak evidence for a specific 2026 content story because the top terms are dominated by historical artifacts and stack tokens. The effect is real, but the interpretation is messy.

The section-filtered comparison is weaker than it looks because of the 72 versus 3,359 imbalance. I would not use it as a standalone headline. Its main value is as a sensitivity check showing that the 2026 AI and workflow vocabulary does not disappear when benefits and company boilerplate are removed.

The raw same-row comparison is a good recall-sensitive diagnostic, not the primary estimator. It is nonetheless important because it demonstrates that the 2026 AI lexicon is visible in the underlying text, not only in derived features. The BERTopic cross-check is the most persuasive corroboration here: topic 1 contains 1,432 docs, with 83.2% from 2026 and top terms ai, ml, workflows, aiml, llms, and qualifications. That is a real 2026 cluster.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text:
RQ2 is still viable, but the current evidence says it needs a two-part framing. The data support both content migration and template drift, but the template drift is not a side note. It is central to interpreting the term shifts.

The original broad "language evolution" framing is too loose. The stronger framing is: SWE postings in 2026 contain a distinct AI/workflow vocabulary, but the observable shift is entangled with historical stack residue, boilerplate, and sectioning differences. That makes the paper less tidy, but more credible.

The best alternative framing is not "AI hype" or "junior decline." It is posting-surface recomposition. A second plausible alternative is that this is mostly a platform/formatting change rather than a demand change. I do not think that alternative is sufficient on its own because the emergent AI terms and the AI/workflow topic are too strong to be dismissed as formatting only. The better interpretation is that both mechanisms operate together, with the AI vocabulary being the substantive part and the template drift being the measurement problem.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text:
The strongest story so far is not just that AI terms entered SWE postings. It is that they entered a text surface that is itself changing shape. The main empirical signal to carry forward is the 2026 AI/workflow cluster, backed by Claude, LLM-based, multi-agent, embeddings, and AI-enabled terms, plus a topic model that isolates an AI-heavy period-skewed cluster.

At the same time, the historical cleaned text still carries a lot of old stack and credential language, and the section-filtered comparison is too sparse on the 2024 side to support a clean symmetric claim. That means the final paper should not frame T12 as a simple before/after word list. It should frame it as a noisy but informative map of how the observable language surface is changing.

docs.title: Research question evolution
docs.text:
RQ2 should be split into two subquestions: 1. Which content bundles became more salient in 2026 SWE postings, especially AI/workflow bundles? 2. Which of those apparent shifts survive section filtering and raw-text sensitivity, and which are artifacts of template drift?

RQ1 should not be read from full cleaned text alone. The current term evidence is too entangled with structure and old-form artifacts to support a simple volume-or-level claim.

docs.title: Gaps and weaknesses
docs.text:
The section-filtered comparison is too imbalanced for a strong headline. That needs to be explicitly labeled as a weak sensitivity rather than treated as equal footing with the full-text comparison.

The cleaned corpus still contains concatenation and artifact tokens in the historical data, which may inflate the historical side of the log-odds ranking. That should be handled in the analysis phase, not ignored.

BERTopic is useful, but it still needs careful interpretation for whether the smaller mobile/platform topics are substantive or just another view of the same surface changes.

docs.title: Direction for next wave
docs.text:
Keep the AI/workflow vocabulary as a candidate lead and pursue it with stricter validation. The most valuable follow-up is to separate genuine AI-content movement from template drift using the section-filtered surface, raw-text recall, and topic-level cross-checks together.

Do not invest heavily in a claim that the section-filtered comparison is a balanced 2024-vs-2026 test. It is not. Treat it as a diagnostic. The core result is the emergence of a 2026 AI/workflow language cluster, not a simple full-text ranking.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: T12 points toward an empirical restructuring paper, but with a very specific caveat: the language signal is real, yet the document surface is unstable. The strongest contribution is the detection of a 2026 AI/workflow vocabulary cluster and the demonstration that its visibility depends on how aggressively boilerplate and template drift are filtered out.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
The strongest finding is that posting length growth is real, but it is not just "more requirements." On the capped LinkedIn SWE corpus, mean cleaned text length rises from 1,969 chars in arshkon 2024-04 and 2,281 in asaniczka 2024-01 to 2,644 in scraped 2026, which is well above the within-2024 baseline difference of 312 chars. The structural story is even sharper: explicit core sections are rare in 2024, but common in 2026. Only 1.6% of arshkon docs and 4.5% of asaniczka docs have any detected core section, versus 60.8% and 62.2% in the two scraped periods.

That means the length shift is not just textual inflation. It is also a formatting shift. In the section composition table, historical postings are overwhelmingly unclassified by the parser (98.2% arshkon, 95.3% asaniczka), while scraped postings allocate a much larger share of characters to role_summary, responsibilities, requirements, and preferred sections. Core sections plus preferred/benefits/about-company/legal account for roughly 1.7% of arshkon text, 4.3% of asaniczka text, and 37.8% to 38.0% of scraped text. The posting template itself changed materially.

The LLM-text surface is also large enough to matter on the scraped side, but not uniform. In the current text-source counts, scraped 2026-03 has 3,979 rows labeled llm and 15,817 labeled raw; scraped 2026-04 has 1,987 llm rows and 15,369 raw rows. That is why the primary text-sensitive analyses should stay on text_source = 'llm', with raw text reserved for recall-sensitive checks.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
The biggest surprise is how thin the explicit section signal is in the historical snapshots. That is not a parser failure. It is evidence that most 2024 postings do not expose a stable sectional anatomy, so any "requirements vs boilerplate" analysis has to accept that the earlier corpus is mostly free text.

The other surprise is that cleaned text is consistently harder to read than raw text. In the readability calibration sample, cleaned FK grade is around 20.9 to 21.6 versus 17.6 to 18.1 raw, and cleaned average sentence length is roughly 29 to 32 words versus 24 to 26 raw. The cleaned corpus is not a simpler, more canonical surface; it is a denser one.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
The section parser is moderate-evidence infrastructure: transparent, regex-based, and easy to audit, but coarse. Its main value is comparative, not exhaustive. The coverage result is strong enough to matter because the historical-vs-scraped gap is large and stable across both 2026 months.

The length and tone statistics are stronger evidence because they use the capped corpus broadly. The within-2024 calibration matters here. For example, the calibration ratio for cleaned text length is 2.16, while the ratio for core share is 13.6 and for section_chars_requirements 48.8. Those are not subtle changes. They are large enough that a pure noise explanation is implausible.

Readability is moderate evidence because it comes from a 7,894-row sample, but the sampled pattern is internally coherent: entry-level scraped postings are shorter and somewhat easier to read than mid-senior scraped postings, yet both are materially longer and structurally richer than 2024 counterparts. In scraped 2026-03, entry postings average 2,253 chars with mean core_share 0.439 and mean sentence length 27.6, while mid-senior postings average 2,856 chars with mean core_share 0.371 and mean sentence length 31.6. The same pattern holds in 2026-04.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text:
RQ1 as written is only partially supported by T13. The evidence does not yet justify a junior-rung headline. What it does support is a broader posting-restructuring claim: SWE ads became longer, more explicitly sectioned, and more variable in how much space they devote to role content versus boilerplate and company language.

RQ2 is strengthened, but only as a decomposition question. The right question is not whether "requirements grew" in the abstract. It is which sections expanded, and whether that expansion is concentrated in responsibilities/requirements or spread across marketing, legal, and company-description text. On the current evidence, the answer is: both happened, with the structural shift itself being substantial.

The alternative framing that fits the data best is template evolution rather than raw demand inflation. A second plausible framing is requirement expansion, but it is weaker because the parser shows large gains in unclassified-to-structured text and not just in requirement-specific text. I prefer template evolution as the lead interpretation because it explains both the length growth and the section-coverage jump without overclaiming about employer demand.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text:
The posting surface changed materially between 2024 and 2026. The most publishable implication is methodological as much as substantive: later text analyses cannot assume that the same amount of text means the same type of text. The market may have changed, but so did the document form through which the market speaks.

That is a strong constraint for T12 and later stages. The main text signal should come from section-filtered or otherwise normalized surfaces, not from raw length alone. Raw text can still be used for recall-sensitive checks, but it should not be the primary estimate.

docs.title: Research question evolution
docs.text:
RQ1 should be reframed away from "junior share/volume" as the immediate T13 lead. A better current formulation is: how did the structure of SWE postings change between 2024 and 2026, and does that structural change concentrate in core role content or in boilerplate/template sections?

RQ2 should become: which sections of postings expanded or contracted, and how much of the growth is actually requirements/responsibilities content rather than generalized length inflation? This keeps the original intent but makes it compatible with what the data actually show.

docs.title: Gaps and weaknesses
docs.text:
The section classifier misses unmarked structure, so "unclassified" is a real residual category, not pure noise. That limits how sharply we can split boilerplate from content in the historical data.

Readability is only a calibrated sample, not a full-corpus measurement. I would not build a headline claim on small entry-level slices alone. The entry-vs-mid-senior split is suggestive but still subordinate to the broader structural finding.

docs.title: Direction for next wave
docs.text: Use the section spans to isolate the requirement and responsibility surface in T12, but keep a raw-text sensitivity branch for recall. The key test is whether the apparent 2024-to-2026 term shifts survive when boilerplate and section-template drift are stripped away.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: T13 pushes the paper toward a measurement-validity plus restructuring story. The strongest contribution so far is not "longer postings" by itself. It is that the document structure of SWE ads changed enough that any later language-shift claim has to be conditioned on sectioning, not just token counts.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: The technology surface is not just getting broader. It is reorganizing into a stable cloud/frontend backbone plus a much more coherent AI/LLM cluster in the scraped window. The old 2024 data cluster fragments, while llm, generative_ai, agent, claude, and related terms gain dense co-occurrence ties that were barely present in the historical snapshots.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
The broad stack backbone is surprisingly persistent. aws, ci_cd, kubernetes, javascript, typescript, sql, and the frontend/backend families remain central across all three sources. What changes is the texture around that backbone: the scraped source adds a visibly stronger AI tool / LLM community and pulls in adjacent app and ML terms such as fastapi, pandas, scikit_learn, mlflow, kubeflow, and nextjs.

AI-tool mention growth is real, but it is not just a length artifact. In the primary spec, AI-tool / LLM mentions rise from 2.05% of arshkon SWE rows to 17.19% of scraped SWE rows. Excluding aggregators barely moves that share (2.14% to 17.61%). The postings that mention AI tools are also denser in technology mentions, so the signal is both broader and more concentrated, not just longer.

The co-occurrence network supports the same story. With company capping at 30 postings per company per source-period, the largest stable communities are cloud/devops, frontend/web, testing/methodology, and an AI/ML cluster. Under no-cap sensitivity, the same communities remain, which means the result is not an employer-concentration artifact.

The structured skills file is useful, but it is not the same measurement surface as the extracted tech matrix. After aliasing, 167 tech columns overlap and rank correlation is only 0.69. The structured field captures more generic and process-oriented skill language; the regex-extracted matrix is more sensitive to explicit technology tokens. They are complementary, not interchangeable.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
The biggest surprise is how little the seniority signal survives in the structured skill baseline. On asaniczka, the label-based entry vs mid-senior comparison leaves only one FDR-significant skill (pandas), while the YOE-based junior proxy yields a different set with zero overlap. That is a measurement warning, not just a sparsity issue.

Another surprise is that the AI co-occurrence neighborhood is broader than a pure model-stack story. The top associations include generative_ai, nlp, pytorch, but also fastapi, nextjs, typescript, and opentelemetry. The AI tool layer is being wrapped into application delivery, not only into traditional ML work.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
The tech-shift result is strong. The within-2024 calibration table shows that several 2026 gains are far above the arshkon-vs-asaniczka baseline noise: llm, generative_ai, agent, claude, typescript, gcp, and kubernetes all have signal-to-noise ratios comfortably above 2. One important counterexample is c_plus_plus: its 2026 decline is smaller than the 2024 source-to-source difference, so it should not be treated as a robust temporal result.

The network result is also strong because it survives the required sensitivity checks. The company-capped and uncapped networks both preserve the same high-level cluster structure. The only thing that changes materially is the size of the AI and cloud communities, which is itself part of the substantive story.

The structured-vs-extracted validation is moderate evidence. The correlation is decent, but the disagreement is informative rather than negligible. It tells us the structured field is richer on generic skill language, while the regex matrix is better at explicit stack terms. For the paper, that means the structured skills field is a baseline, not a replacement.

The seniority-by-skill result is weak if you insist on seniority_final = entry alone. It is too sparse. The YOE proxy is the more stable view and should be treated as the primary junior comparator for skill-level claims. Material disagreement between the two is itself the result.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion: the AI-tool share moves only slightly from 2.05% to 2.14% in arshkon and from 17.19% to 17.61% in scraped. The direction does not change. See T14_sensitivity_no_aggregators.csv.

Company capping: the co-occurrence network keeps the same cloud/frontend/testing/agile backbone with and without capping. Without the cap, the cloud community grows a bit more and the AI community becomes larger, but the cluster identities do not change. See T14_community_overlap_arshkon_vs_scraped.csv.

Within-2024 calibration: the AI and LLM shifts are well above the arshkon-vs-asaniczka baseline. c_plus_plus and a few generic language changes are not. The right reading is not “everything changed,” but “the new AI stack changed in a way the historical source gap does not explain.”

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text:
The strongest narrative is not generic scope inflation. It is ecosystem reconfiguration: cloud/devops and frontend remain the core, but the scraped window has a more explicit AI/LLM layer that is tied into application and ML tooling. That layer is visible in the network, in the top change table, and in the AI-tool co-occurrence associations.

The structured skills baseline adds a second point: the seniority story is not cleanly legible in skill inventories alone. The label-based junior bucket is too narrow to carry the analysis. For downstream work, the YOE proxy should remain the junior validator whenever skill differences are involved.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
The technology matrix is binary and description-driven, so mention rate does not equal required depth. That matters especially for AI terms, where aspirational mentions can rise faster than actual workflow change.

The structured skills file contains a much broader and noisier vocabulary than the regex tech matrix. That breadth is useful, but it is also why the overlap correlation is only moderate.

The seniority skill analysis is especially fragile under seniority_final = entry. The table is still worth keeping, but only as a high-precision slice, not as the main junior surface.

docs.title: Direction for downstream tasks
docs.text:
Use the tech network result as the paper’s main technology-side structural evidence. The cloud/frontend backbone plus the new AI community is strong enough to matter.

Treat the structured skills file as a validation surface, not a ground truth. It is most useful for checking whether extracted tech trends are missing broader process language or vice versa.

Do not let the sparse explicit-entry bucket define junior skill conclusions. Any junior skill or convergence analysis should keep the YOE proxy in view.

docs.title: T15 Semantic Similarity Landscape & Convergence Analysis
Headline finding
docs.text: There is no clean junior-senior convergence story at the corpus level. The semantic space becomes tighter over time, but junior and senior postings do not collapse into each other. Embeddings show only a tiny change that sits inside the 2024 source gap; TF-IDF shows a clearer divergence. The most defensible reading is tighter templating plus stronger period structure, not seniority convergence.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
Using the full text_source = llm subset gives us 26,219 labeled SWE LinkedIn rows, with no raw fallback contamination in the semantic analysis. That is enough to estimate centroids, dispersion, and nearest neighbors without downsampling the actual inference set. The visual sample is only for plotting.

The strongest structural result is that period slightly dominates seniority. Across the centroid matrices, same-period groups are a bit more similar than same-seniority groups across periods: 0.9516 vs 0.9441 under embeddings, and 0.8623 vs 0.8416 under TF-IDF. The gap is modest, but it is consistently on the period side.

The convergence check is negative. Under embeddings, junior-senior similarity is 0.9801 in asaniczka, 0.9649 in arshkon, and 0.9618 in scraped. The arshkon-to-scraped change is smaller than the asaniczka-to-arshkon baseline gap, so it does not survive calibration. Under TF-IDF, the same similarity falls from 0.9383 to 0.9186 to 0.8676, which is a larger drop than the 2024 baseline and points to divergence rather than convergence.

The YOE proxy tells the same basic story, but with even higher absolute similarity because the junior/senior split is broader. yoe_extracted <= 2 vs >= 3 stays very close in every source, yet the scraped change still does not outrun the 2024 baseline. So the proxy does not rescue a convergence claim.

Within-group dispersion drops in 2026. That means postings are getting more internally homogeneous even while junior and senior remain distinct. The cleanest interpretation is not “junior and senior are merging,” but “the language is standardizing and the semantic space is tightening.”

Nearest neighbors reinforce the same boundary-blur story. For 2026 entry posts, the 5-nearest 2024 neighbors are mostly senior and unknown rows. Junior neighbors appear more often than the 2024 base rate, but only modestly: 4.35% vs a 1.60% base under embeddings, and 2.88% vs 1.60% under TF-IDF.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
The biggest surprise is the representation disagreement. Embeddings and TF-IDF agree on the direction of the main finding, but not on magnitude. Embeddings are very conservative and almost flatten the cross-period change; TF-IDF sees the gap widen more clearly. That is a real methodological signal, not just a nuisance.

The other surprise is that the 2026 space becomes tighter while the junior-senior gap does not close. That combination usually means templating, standardization, or stronger employer signaling, not genuine convergence of role content.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
The embedding result is moderate evidence. The group sizes are adequate for senior and unknown rows, but junior and mid are small, especially in scraped 2026-04. The tiny absolute change and the calibration gap mean I would not elevate embeddings alone into a headline.

The TF-IDF result is stronger as an empirical signal because the change is larger than the 2024 baseline difference. It still needs caution because TF-IDF is more sensitive to boilerplate and wording, but the sign is consistent with the embedding view: no convergence.

The nearest-neighbor result is moderate evidence. It is conceptually clean and easy to explain, but it is still neighborhood-based rather than causal. Its value is in showing where the 2026 entry postings live in the older semantic space.

The YOE-proxy check is important but descriptive. It confirms that the negative convergence result is not just an artifact of seniority_final, but the proxy itself is broad enough that it should not be over-read.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
Aggregator exclusion: the source-level junior-senior similarity barely moves. Under embeddings, arshkon changes from 0.9649 to 0.9632 and scraped from 0.9618 to 0.9596. Under TF-IDF, arshkon moves from 0.9186 to 0.9100 and scraped from 0.8676 to 0.8652. The conclusion does not change.

Within-2024 calibration: the 2026 shift does not exceed the historical source gap under embeddings, and it does under TF-IDF. That is why the correct conclusion is not “strong convergence.” At best, embeddings show a tiny change inside noise; TF-IDF shows divergence.

Seniority operationalization: the YOE proxy gives the same qualitative answer. It does not produce a convincing convergence signal, and it keeps junior-senior similarity extremely high in every source. That makes the boundary look sticky rather than collapsing.

Source restriction: the source-level tables show the same pattern as the period-level tables, which means the result is not driven by one snapshot window. The 2024 baseline already differs across arshkon and asaniczka, so that baseline has to stay visible in any downstream claim.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
The mid group is tiny in every period, especially after embedding filtering. I treat it as descriptive, not as the basis for any strong claim.

The outlier tables are dominated by noisy, legacy, or highly specialized postings. Examples include 5G/embedded roles, TS/SCI security work, and malformed descriptions with no usable cleaned text. That is useful because it shows the semantic map is catching real fringe structure, but it also means the tail is noisy.

There are no T09 archetype labels in the shared artifacts, so I could not run the domain-stratified convergence version. That is the main missing follow-up if we want to ask whether AI/ML roles converge differently from frontend or embedded roles.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text:
The right story is not convergence. It is standardization plus recomposition. The semantic space tightens, AI/tool language becomes more coherent, and the dominant variation is slightly more period-linked than seniority-linked. Seniority boundaries remain visible, but they are not the main axis of change.

That makes the paper stronger, not weaker. It shifts the analysis away from a fragile “junior and senior are becoming the same” claim and toward a more defensible account of how the posting language itself is reorganizing.

docs.title: Direction for downstream analysis
docs.text:
Deprioritize a simple convergence framing in the paper. The stronger story is that role language is becoming more standardized while the seniority boundary remains intact.

If we need a junior-focused figure later, it should lean on the YOE proxy and nearest-neighbor excess, not on the explicit entry label alone.

If domain labels become available, the next useful question is whether this non-convergence differs by archetype. The current data say the overall corpus does not converge; they do not yet tell us whether ML/AI, frontend, and embedded move differently.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: Returning companies do not split into a single “AI adopters versus everyone else” axis. On the primary overlap panel, 237 companies appear in both arshkon and scraped LinkedIn with at least 3 SWE postings on each side, and 122 clear the >=5 robustness cut. K-means on the company-level change vectors picks four useful trajectories: stack expansion (100 companies), template inflation / text-heavy (52), entry-heavy compact (49), and AI-forward recomposition (36). The strongest aggregate signal is within-company growth in AI/LLM language, scope language, and cleaned-text length; the junior story is the most instrument-sensitive piece.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
The company panel is large enough to support a real typology, but only if we treat text carefully. In the primary overlap panel, 2024 company-year rows are almost entirely llm-cleaned (llm_text_share_2024 = 0.995), while the 2026 side is mostly raw fallback (llm_text_share_2026 = 0.140). The llm-only overlap shrinks to 113 companies, so the strict text subset is a sensitivity check, not the main frame.

The robust company-level changes are the same ones Wave 2 already pointed to: AI/LLM mention share rises by 14.0 pp, scope share by 18.8 pp, cleaned-text length by 802 chars, and requirement breadth by 1.39 categories on the primary panel. Most of that movement is within-company. In the overlap decomposition, AI share is mostly within-company (+0.136 of +0.140), scope is mostly within-company (+0.173 of +0.188), and cleaned-text length is also mostly within-company (+593 of +802 chars). Tech count moves much less (+0.82), which is why this is not simply a “more tech” story.

The company clusters are interpretable. AI-forward recomposition companies look like ServiceNow, Procore, Macquarie Group, Infojini, and KPG99: AI/LLM share and domain share rise sharply, and requirement breadth expands. stack expansion companies such as Charter Global, ICE, Photon, and Visa look more like classic credential-stack inflation. template inflation / text-heavy companies such as Pinterest, Workday, Netflix, AT&T, and ByteDance add a lot of text without the same AI/domain jump. entry-heavy compact companies such as Anduril, OSI Engineering, TekWissen, Themesoft, and TikTok remain shorter and lower-tech but tilt more junior.

The entry story splits by instrument. On the primary arshkon-only overlap panel, explicit seniority_final = entry falls by 0.8 pp, while the YOE proxy rises by 5.7 pp. Under the cap-25 sensitivity, explicit entry falls by 1.0 pp and the YOE proxy rises by 0.9 pp. Under pooled 2024, explicit entry flips sign and rises by 1.4 pp, while the YOE proxy rises by 7.6 pp. That is not a cosmetic difference. It is the main measurement warning in this task.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
The pooled-2024 baseline matters more than expected. Using arshkon alone, the explicit-entry trend is mildly negative. Using pooled 2024, it becomes positive. The within-company AI/scope/length story survives all of those choices, but the junior claim does not. That is exactly the kind of instrument dependence the exploration was supposed to surface.

The domain story is also more nuanced than a clean “AI replaced everything else” narrative. At the family level, AI / LLM share rises, but so do Embedded and DevOps shares, while Frontend / Mobile, Data / Platform, and Requirements / Workflow all lose share. That reads more like recomposition toward AI plus infra/embedded than a one-dimensional collapse into AI jobs.

The domain decomposition is informative for explicit entry, but it is not clean for the YOE proxy. For entry_final, the sequential domain split is close, with only a small residual. For entry_yoe, the residual is large and nearly as large as the total change. I would not pretend that the domain table fully explains the YOE junior rise.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
AI-forward recomposition and stack expansion are the strongest company-strategy classes. They sit on large company counts, survive the no-aggregator and cap-25 cuts, and line up with the verified Wave 2 signals.

template inflation / text-heavy is also credible, but it should be framed as a posting-format trajectory as much as a hiring strategy. It is a reminder that longer descriptions do not always mean more substantive requirements.

entry-heavy compact is the most measurement-sensitive class. It is real, but it leans hardest on the explicit-vs-YOE split and therefore should be described as a junior-leaning compact strategy, not as a clean junior-hiring boom.

The no-aggregator and cap-25 sensitivities are reassuring. The sign of the key company-level changes does not move, and the magnitudes stay in the same ballpark. The llm-only overlap is much thinner, so it should remain a check rather than a headline.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text:
The initial RQ1 framing is only partly right here. The data do not support a simple junior-collapse story at the company level. They support a more mixed story: returning firms are widening requirement stacks and AI language within their own postings, while the junior signal depends heavily on how we measure it.

The strongest alternative framing is company strategy recomposition under template drift. I prefer that over “junior roles disappeared” because it survives the verified AI/scope/length signals and does not require the unstable entry label to do all the work.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text: The company-level story is not that every employer became AI-heavy. It is that firms diverged into a few strategies. Some companies added AI/LLM language and changed the domain mix; others mainly thickened the stack; others inflated text without changing the underlying technical profile much; and a smaller group stayed compact but skewed more junior-like.

docs.title: Research question evolution
docs.text:
RQ1 should be narrowed here to something like: How did returning SWE employers change their posting strategy between 2024 and 2026, and how much of that change was within-company versus company composition?

That phrasing keeps the measurement issue visible and avoids over-committing to a single junior-share narrative. The pooled-2024 sensitivity makes that revision necessary.

docs.title: Gaps and weaknesses
docs.text: The llm-only overlap panel is thin, so text-heavy claims should always acknowledge the raw-fallback majority in 2026. The YOE domain decomposition is still too residual-heavy to be treated as a solved explanation. And the company clusters are heuristic, not latent truth.

docs.title: Direction for downstream analysis
docs.text: Use the company typology as a filter for the next wave: follow the AI-forward recomposition and stack expansion firms into the cross-occupation and ghost-check tasks, and treat the entry-heavy compact firms as the best place to probe the junior measurement split.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: If we stopped here, the paper would still be an empirical restructuring paper, but the lead would be company strategy recomposition, not junior collapse. The methods angle is now central because the pooled-2024 sensitivity changes the entry conclusion while leaving the AI/scope story intact.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: Geography matters, but it does not rescue a clean “junior collapse” story. After excluding multi-location rows, only 17 metros clear the >=50 postings-in-both-periods threshold, and the remote bucket is dead: is_remote_inferred is 0 in every selected metro in scraped 2026. Across those metros, AI/LLM language, scope language, and cleaned-text length all rise broadly, but the relation between AI growth and entry change depends on the seniority instrument. Explicit seniority_final entry declines where AI grows fastest; the YOE proxy does not tell the same story.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
The metro frame is usable but narrow. The primary sample excludes 3,007 multi-location SWE postings and 11,307 SWE rows with no metro at all. Of the remaining metro-coded rows, 17 metros clear >=50 postings in both arshkon and scraped. That is enough for a descriptive geography section, not enough for a grand regional theory.

AI / LLM growth is broad, not just coastal. The biggest increases in AI / LLM family share show up in Houston (+11.1 pp), Dallas-Fort Worth (+9.6), Chicago (+9.3), Boston (+8.8), Austin (+8.8), Charlotte (+8.3), Philadelphia (+8.2), and Seattle (+7.8). That means the AI re-centering is not confined to the Bay Area or Seattle, even though those metros also rise.

Scope inflation is geographically widespread. The strongest scope gains are San Diego (+26.6 pp), Phoenix (+23.7), San Francisco (+23.2), Minneapolis (+22.9), Atlanta (+22.0), Austin (+21.8), and Chicago (+21.4). The pattern looks like a broad posting-language shift rather than a small number of hubs rewriting the market for everyone else.

The entry signal is instrument-sensitive at the metro level. Under seniority_final, explicit entry falls most in Phoenix (-5.6 pp), Houston (-5.6), Charlotte (-5.5), Atlanta (-3.5), and San Francisco (-2.2). It rises in Raleigh (+4.0), Chicago (+2.1), Detroit (+1.6), Philadelphia (+1.5), and Los Angeles (+1.3). Under the YOE proxy, the leaders are different: San Diego (+10.5 pp), Philadelphia (+10.4), San Francisco (+8.7), Los Angeles (+7.6), Denver (+5.2), Seattle (+4.8), and Boston (+3.4) rise, while Detroit (-12.8), Houston (-7.0), Austin (-5.8), Charlotte (-4.3), and Minneapolis (-3.8) fall.

The AI-entry relationship also depends on the instrument. Across metros, delta_ai_any_share correlates negatively with delta_entry_final_share (r = -0.35, Spearman -0.44), but only weakly and positively with delta_entry_yoe_share (r = 0.19, Spearman 0.04). So the geography does not stabilize the seniority measurement problem; it makes it more visible.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
Remote work is not a usable geography dimension here. Every selected metro has remote share 0 in scraped 2026, which means the remote field is either uninformative or missing in a way that is too systematic to use. I would treat that as a data artifact, not a substantive null finding.

The geography of AI/domain recomposition is broader than expected. The strongest AI / LLM family gains are not concentrated only in SF and Seattle. Houston, Dallas-Fort Worth, Chicago, Boston, Austin, Charlotte, and Philadelphia all sit near the top. That is a stronger market-recomposition signal than a pure coastal-hub narrative would predict.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
The metro-level change tables are strong enough for descriptive claims because the selected panel is balanced on both periods and the no-aggregator and cap-25 sensitivities preserve the sign of the main changes. The caveat is that metro-level correlations are still just 17 points, so they are suggestive rather than causal.

The sensitivity summary is reassuring. No-aggregator and cap-25 versions are close to the primary on the main metrics, with mean absolute differences that are small relative to the absolute level of the shifts. The fragile piece is again the YOE proxy on entry: some metro sign flips appear under the no-aggregator cut, which is another reminder that the proxy is useful but not a clean identity with seniority_final.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text:
The geography evidence weakens any hub-only or coastal-only framing. It also does not support a clean geography-first paper. The more defensible story is that the posting language shift is broad across metros, while the seniority interpretation remains instrument-dependent.

The best alternative framing is “broad regional recomposition with uneven intensity.” I prefer that over “AI jobs moved to a few hubs” because the AI / LLM growth appears across Texas, the Midwest, the Northeast, and the West at the same time.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text: Metro structure is a modifier, not the main mechanism. Some cities show bigger explicit-entry declines, some show bigger YOE-junior gains, and some show both AI and scope expansion without a clear seniority story. The core market-restructuring narrative is still company- and template-driven; geography mostly tells us where the effect is stronger or weaker.

docs.title: Research question evolution
docs.text:
Geography should stay secondary. The useful question is not “which city lost juniors,” but:

RQ1 adjunct: Are AI/LLM and scope changes geographically concentrated, or are they broadly distributed across SWE labor markets?

That is a better fit for this dataset than a standalone regional labor-market claim.

docs.title: Gaps and weaknesses
docs.text: is_remote_inferred is not usable here because the remote rate is identically zero in the selected metro frame. The metro work also excludes a large no-metro and multi-location pool, so it cannot describe the whole platform surface. Finally, the metro sample is only 17 cities, so correlation claims should stay modest.

docs.title: Direction for downstream analysis
docs.text: Use the geography results as context and as a robustness layer, not as the lead. The next wave should still prioritize cross-occupation specificity, company strategy, and the ghost/aspirational requirement checks.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: Geography is a supporting section, not the headline. Its main value is that it shows the AI/scope shift is broad across metros and that the explicit-entry result is not just a Bay Area artifact. The stronger lead remains the company strategy recomposition story from T16.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: The occupation boundary shifts, but it does not collapse. SWE-adjacent vs SWE TF-IDF centroid similarity stays high at 0.80 in arshkon, 0.83 in asaniczka, 0.82 in scraped March, and 0.82 in scraped April. The stronger change is not wholesale convergence; it is that AI-tool language, scope language, and description length all rise across SWE, adjacent, and control groups, with SWE still carrying the densest bundle.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text: The cleanest SWE-specific signal is AI-tool language. In the cleaned-text frame, AI-tool share rises from 1.98% to 20.38% for SWE, from 2.45% to 18.14% for adjacent roles, and only from 1.25% to 1.41% for controls. The broader template signal is real too. Raw median description length rises from 2,974 to 4,891 characters in SWE, from 3,599 to 5,136 in adjacent roles, and from 3,096 to 4,130 in controls. Raw scope share also rises in all three groups, including controls. SWE still has the densest requirements bundle. In the cleaned-text frame, SWE posts have higher tech density than adjacent or control roles in both periods, and the combined requirement-breadth signal is highest in SWE. The migration vocabulary is more about analytic and coordination language than a hard coding/noncoding split. Terms like scientist, data scientist, data science, sql, analytics, model, communicate, and stakeholders move closer to SWE-adjacent roles. Seniority remains a measurement issue, not a clean boundary result. Explicit entry is tiny under seniority_final, while the YOE proxy is materially broader in every occupation group.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text: The control group is not a zero line. It also gets longer and somewhat more AI- and scope-heavy in the raw-text sensitivity tables. The centroid similarity is high rather than low. That means adjacent and SWE already share a substantial common vocabulary surface; the change is in density and emphasis, not a sharp semantic wall.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text: The AI-tool result is strong. It survives the cleaned-text frame, the raw-text sensitivity table, aggregator exclusion, and title_lookup_llm exclusion with only small movement. The scope and length results are moderate but real. They are visible in both cleaned and raw text, but the raw-text view makes the field-wide template shift clearer than the cleaned-text view. The similarity analysis is moderate. The similarities are stable and high, so the result is not a collapse story; it is a porosity story. The sample is company-capped at 25 postings per company per source-period for the boundary text work, which reduces domination by a few prolific employers.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text: RQ1 weakens if it is framed as a pure SWE-only restructuring story. The data support a stronger alternative: SWE is being densified inside a broader technical-posting template shift. RQ2 strengthens, but the best framing is not “requirements moved once.” It is that AI/workflow language, scope language, and stacked requirements diffuse across adjacent technical roles while remaining densest in SWE. RQ3 is untouched by this wave.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text: The market is not converging into one occupation. It is converging on a more standardized way of describing technical work, and SWE sits at the dense end of that standardized surface. The paper should not claim collapse. It should claim differential densification: SWE changes most, but the surrounding technical labor market changes in the same direction.

docs.title: Research question evolution
docs.text: RQ1 should become: how much of the 2024-to-2026 change is SWE-specific densification versus field-wide posting-template evolution? RQ2 should ask which requirement bundles migrate across occupation boundaries, especially AI-tool language, scope language, and tech-stack adjacency. The old “junior collapse” reading should remain a secondary possibility, not the lead framing.

docs.title: Gaps and weaknesses
docs.text: The biggest gap is that cleaned-text coverage is thinner in scraped 2026, especially outside SWE. The similarity analysis is a snapshot comparison, not a continuous trajectory. It tells us about the shape of the language surface, not about the underlying work itself.

docs.title: Direction for next wave
docs.text: The next wave should connect this boundary recomposition story to mechanism checks. The highest-value follow-ups are ghost-forensics, employer-requirement divergence, and within-firm versus between-firm decomposition.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: The paper is strongest as an empirical restructuring study with a posting-template caveat. If we stopped here, the lead result would be: SWE postings are not collapsing into adjacent roles, but the technical posting surface is standardizing around AI, scope, and denser requirements, with SWE still the densest bundle.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: This is not a continuous series. It is two historical snapshots plus a growing scraped window, and the rate story only makes sense at that level. The clearest change is AI-tool language and requirement stacking: in the cleaned-text frame, AI-tool share rises from 2.56% in arshkon to 20.30% in scraped 2026, and requirement-breadth rises from 5.94 to 6.98. Raw description length also jumps from 2,974 to 4,891 characters. Scope is real too, but the cleaned-text scope proxy is too sparse to use as a lead result; the raw-text sensitivity shows the useful signal.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text: The observed source windows are uneven by design: asaniczka is 2024-01-12 to 2024-01-17, arshkon is 2024-04-05 to 2024-04-20, and scraped is 2026-03-19 to 2026-04-09. The seniority gap remains a measurement issue. Explicit entry is only 0.87% in asaniczka, 3.73% in arshkon, and 2.80% in scraped, while the YOE<=2 share is materially larger at 7.34%, 9.29%, and 11.65%. The source-summary view is more trustworthy than a smooth time-series story. Cleaned-text coverage is 99.7% in arshkon, 85.9% in asaniczka, and only 16.1% in scraped, so the labeled-text rates in 2026 are thin and must be read as such. The raw sensitivity makes the scope signal usable. Raw scope share rises from 38.9%-39.5% in the historical snapshots to 59.5% in scraped, which is consistent with the broader template-shift story from T18.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text: The daily scraped window is batchy, but not stale. The first day is the largest scrape day, yet posting_age_days is 1.0 on every scraped day, so this looks more like scrape cadence or platform formatting than a backlog of old postings. The late-April daily points are very thin on labeled text. By 2026-04-07 through 2026-04-10, labeled counts fall to 3-4 rows per day, so the AI spikes there are noise, not signal.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text: AI-tool language is the strongest rate result. It survives the 2024 calibration gap and accelerates sharply into 2026; the annualized cross-period change is 0.090 versus 0.031 within 2024, an acceleration ratio of 2.90. Requirement breadth is also strong. It rises more in the 2026 window than in the 2024 calibration gap, with an acceleration ratio of 1.91. Description length is directionally consistent but noisier. The raw-text rise is clear, while the cleaned-text length series is less stable because the labeled subset changes across windows. Scope is a valid sensitivity result, not a cleaned-text lead metric. The cleaned-text extractor returns zero everywhere, so scope should be cited from the raw-text sensitivity only. The internal arshkon date-bin check is useful as a noise floor: AI share, entry share, YOE<=2 share, and median text length are fairly flat within 2024, which means the large 2026 move is not just ordinary within-window drift.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text: RQ1 is weakened in its original explicit-label form and should be split from the YOE proxy. RQ2 is strengthened if it is reframed around rate acceleration and requirement stacking, not just movement of individual keywords. RQ3 is not answered here.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text: The market changed in windows, not as a smooth line. The substantive change is a denser posting template with much more AI language and more stacked requirements, while the observed daily scrape variation is mostly a measurement issue. The right language is not “continuous trend.” It is “discrete windows with a real step-up in the 2026 window.”

docs.title: Research question evolution
docs.text: RQ1 should explicitly separate seniority_final from the YOE proxy, because they answer different questions. RQ2 should ask which requirement categories accelerate after 2024 relative to the within-2024 baseline. Scope belongs in the question set, but only as a raw-text sensitivity unless the cleaned extractor is improved.

docs.title: Gaps and weaknesses
docs.text: Scraped text coverage is the largest limitation. The daily series is also sparse enough late in the window that some apparent spikes are not interpretable. The analysis still cannot cleanly separate platform formatting changes from employer behavior. The flat one-day posting-age distribution argues against a backlog story, but it does not fully explain the batching.

docs.title: Direction for next wave
docs.text: The next wave should treat the batchy scrape surface as a validity issue and move straight into mechanism checks: ghost requirements, employer-requirement versus worker-usage divergence, and any within-firm decomposition that can distinguish posting-template change from real demand change.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: The paper remains an empirical restructuring study, but T19 adds the key measurement constraint that the 2026 evidence lives in windows, not a smooth panel. If we stopped here, the lead temporal claim would be: AI-tool language and requirement breadth rose materially faster after 2024 than the within-2024 calibration gap, but the 2026 observations must be read as windowed snapshots with thin late-window label coverage.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: The seniority ladder is measurable, but it is not uniformly sharp. The clearest boundary is associate -> mid-senior; the weakest is entry -> associate, especially in 2024 raw text. The explicit junior label remains conservative relative to the YOE proxy, so the junior-like pool is larger than seniority_final = entry.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
• On the LinkedIn SWE frame with known seniority, explicit entry is 4.5% of rows, while yoe_extracted <= 2 is 6.1%.
  • The raw-text boundary model is strongest for associate -> mid-senior in both periods, with mid-senior -> director next and entry -> associate weakest in 2024.
  • The cleaned-text sensitivity keeps the same ordering, but it makes the entry boundary look less random in 2024 and slightly softens the top boundary in 2026.

docs.title: Boundary discriminability
docs.text:
• Raw entry -> associate: AUC 0.505 in 2024 and 0.718 in 2026.
  • Raw associate -> mid-senior: AUC 0.764 in 2024 and 0.773 in 2026.
  • Raw mid-senior -> director: AUC 0.707 in 2024 and 0.665 in 2026.
  • Cleaned sensitivity: entry -> associate 0.658 -> 0.712, associate -> mid-senior 0.724 -> 0.761, mid-senior -> director 0.703 -> 0.599.

docs.title: Missing middle
docs.text: associate is thin and mixed. In mean feature space it sits closer to entry than to mid-senior in both periods, but the classifier boundary to mid-senior is much more stable than the boundary to entry. That makes associate a transition label, not a clean junior proxy and not a clean mid-level bridge.

docs.title: Feature drivers
docs.text: The strongest separators are yoe_extracted, log_description_length, scope_density, tech_count, ai_any, and education_level. Management density is weaker than the other features and should not be treated as the main boundary signal.

docs.title: Domain-stratified boundary analysis
docs.text: The domain slice is only partially identifiable. In 2026, mid-senior -> director is modelable for AI/LLM, Frontend, Data, and Infra; Embedded is too thin. Data is the sharpest of the modelable domains (AUC 0.720), then Infra (0.688), AI/LLM (0.674), and Frontend (0.628). In 2024, almost all domain cells are too sparse except Embedded and one weak Data estimate, so domain-level blur/sharpening should stay cautious.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
• Aggregator exclusion does not change the boundary ordering.
  • The cleaned-text subset is smaller, but it does not reverse the main result.
  • The junior comparison must travel with the YOE proxy; seniority_final is the strict explicit subset, not the full junior-like pool.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text: The initial junior-collapse framing is still not the best story. The more defensible reading is that the explicit seniority ladder remains usable, but the junior side is conservative and the middle boundary is the stable one. If the paper needs a seniority claim, it should lead with measurement asymmetry rather than disappearance.

docs.title: Current positioning
docs.text: T20 is a validity and boundary-structure result. It supports using seniority_final as the explicit label backbone, but it also says junior analyses must keep the YOE proxy beside it. The main downstream implication is that associate should be treated as a thin transition class, not as a substitute for entry.

docs.title: T21 Senior Role Evolution Deep Dive
Headline finding
docs.text: Senior SWE roles are changing, but not in the simple “management out, orchestration in” way. The clean signal is AI-enabled technical orchestration and AI/domain strategy. Management language is mostly stable in the strict set, and the broad-management family is too generic to lead.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
• The strict profile validation is usable: management is mostly manage, mentor, hire, and supervise with grow as the weakest edge term; orchestration is mostly system_design, code_review, agentic, and technical_direction; strategic is mostly stakeholder, roadmap, revenue, and prioritization.
  • Broad management is the noisy family. lead, team, and leadership dominate the broad sample and should stay in sensitivity only.
  • A 3-cluster solution is the best fit by silhouette. It gives a people_manager cluster, an AI/tool_orchestrator cluster, and an AI-domain strategist cluster.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
• Senior rows analyzed: 28,891, with 4,209 aggregators.
  • AI mention is common in the senior frame: 12.5% overall, 11.0% AI-tool, 4.0% AI-domain.
  • Strict management density rises or stays flat across periods: mid-senior 0.377 -> 0.379, director 0.365 -> 0.388.
  • Strict orchestration rises sharply: mid-senior 0.030 -> 0.097, director 0.042 -> 0.083.
  • Strict strategic scope rises more modestly: mid-senior 0.072 -> 0.095, director 0.123 -> 0.117.

docs.title: Senior sub-archetypes
docs.text:
• people_manager: 97.6% of senior postings in 2024, 78.5% in 2026.
  • tech_orchestrator: 1.2% in 2024, 15.3% in 2026.
  • strategist / AI-domain strategist: 1.2% in 2024, 6.3% in 2026.

The new senior shape is therefore not a brand-new fourth archetype. It is the growth of an AI/tool-orchestrator cluster and a smaller AI-domain strategist cluster inside a still-dominant people-manager base.

docs.title: AI interaction
docs.text: AI-mentioning senior postings lean hard toward orchestration in 2026: strict orchestration density is 0.226 for AI posts versus 0.062 for non-AI posts. Management is slightly lower in the AI set, while strategic scope is slightly higher. That looks like domain recomposition, not a simple people-management replacement.

docs.title: Director specifically
docs.text: Directors are still a small group, but they are the clearest AI-signal group in 2026: AI-any jumps from 1.0% in 2024 to 25.3% in 2026. Their strict orchestration density roughly doubles, while strict management is slightly higher, not lower. The director signal therefore supports “AI-heavy seniority” more than “management decline.”

docs.title: Cross-seniority management comparison
docs.text: The full seniority comparison does not support a downward-migration story. Strict management density rises at entry (0.322 -> 0.365), is flat at mid-senior (0.377 -> 0.379), and rises at director (0.365 -> 0.388). Broad management declines, but that family is generic and should not lead.

docs.title: Cleaned-text sensitivity
docs.text: The cleaned subset reinforces the same direction rather than reversing it. In the cleaned sample, mid-senior and director both rise in strict management, orchestration, and strategic scope, with orchestration the biggest move. That makes the AI-orchestration story more durable, not less.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text: The initial senior-archetype-shift hypothesis is only partially supported. The data do not show a clean collapse of people management. What they do show is AI/tool orchestration and AI-domain strategy growing inside senior roles, alongside a stable or slightly rising strict management signal. The strongest framing is therefore recomposition, not substitution.

docs.title: Alternative framings
docs.text:
1. Management decline: weak, because strict management does not fall and entry-level management rises too.
  2. Platform/template drift: real, but insufficient on its own, because the AI/tool and AI-domain clusters expand in a structured way.
  3. Domain recomposition: strongest, because AI posts and senior clusters both shift toward orchestration and AI-specific work.

docs.title: Current positioning
docs.text: T21 should demote “senior role redefinition away from management” as a lead claim. The better story is that senior SWE postings are becoming more AI-aware and orchestration-heavy, with management language remaining present and the senior space splitting into a small AI/tool-orchestrator cluster and a smaller AI-domain strategist cluster.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
• Use strict management only; keep broad management as sensitivity.
  • Treat the AI/tool-orchestrator cluster as the closest thing to a new senior type.
  • Carry the director table and cross-seniority management comparison into synthesis.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text: The strongest signal is not outright impossible credentials. It is a combination of kitchen-sink bundling, aspirational wording, and template repetition. On the section-filtered LLM core frame, AI-mentioning postings have a higher hedge-to-firm ratio than non-AI postings (0.73 vs 0.52 aggregate), and that gap survives the raw full-text sensitivity (1.00 vs 0.80). Direct employers are slightly more ghost-like on the composite score than aggregators, but aggregators are more aspirational on the hedge/firm ratio.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Primary analysis uses LinkedIn SWE only, llm-labeled core sections from t13_section_spans.parquet (role_summary, responsibilities, requirements, preferred). I use the shared tech matrix for distinct technology counts, then layer on:

  • kitchen_sink_product = tech_count × scope_count
  • aspiration ratio = hedging terms / firm requirement terms
  • YOE-scope mismatch = explicit entry plus yoe_extracted >= 5 or >= 3 senior-scope terms
  • credential impossibility = degree contradictions and “no experience” versus long-YOE contradictions
  • template saturation = mean pairwise cosine similarity of core-section text within company

The shared artifact validated_mgmt_patterns.json keeps scope_strict, senior_scope, and management_strong as primary patterns and demotes management_broad to sensitivity only.

docs.title: What we learned
docs.text:
• The primary ghost-like cases are “more of everything” postings, not pure seniority lies. The top entry-level examples include clear YOE/scope tension, but the more common signal is heavy bundling of scope, tech, and AI language.
  • AI language is more aspirational than traditional language in aggregate. In the core-section frame, AI postings show a 0.73 hedge/firm ratio versus 0.52 for non-AI postings; on the raw full-text sensitivity the gap remains and widens (1.00 vs 0.80).
  • Template saturation is real, not just a staffing-agency artifact. Six companies clear the >0.8 mean-pairwise-cosine threshold on the primary core-section frame, including large direct employers such as Walmart, VAST Data, Ashley Furniture Industries, and West Pharmaceutical Services, plus obvious template-driven vendors like Synectic Solutions and Lorvenk.
  • The composite ghost score is not dominated by aggregators. Direct employers are a bit more ghost-like overall (0.185 vs 0.128), even though aggregators have a higher aspiration ratio.

docs.title: What surprised us
docs.text:
• The hard YOE mismatch flag is rare in the cleaned core frame. Many “ghost-like” postings are not invalid on a literal credential basis; they are overloaded, templated, or aspirational instead.
  • The companies with the highest template similarity are not all obvious staffing intermediaries. Some are large direct employers with repeated, highly standardized posting templates.
  • In raw full text, AI-posting aspiration can look stronger than in the section-filtered core frame. That means boilerplate and document form still matter, but they do not erase the AI-versus-non-AI gap.

docs.title: Evidence assessment
docs.text:
• AI aspiration gap: strong. It appears in both the section-filtered core frame and the raw full-text sensitivity.
  • Template saturation: moderate-to-strong. The top companies are easy to interpret, but the fit is sensitive to repetitive templates and the 3-posting floor.
  • YOE-scope mismatch: weak-to-moderate. It exists, but it is sparse.
  • Credential impossibility: weak. Useful as a hard validity flag, but not the main story.
  • Broad management patterns: weak. The validation artifact keeps them only as sensitivity.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
• Aggregator exclusion: direct employers are slightly more ghost-like overall, but aggregators show higher aspiration ratios.
  • Text source: the AI aspiration gap survives raw full text.
  • Company saturation floor: I require at least 3 postings per company; that removes trivial 2-post duplicates and leaves 280 companies in the saturation table.
  • Management validation: broad management stays out of the primary score, per the shared JSON.

docs.title: Narrative evaluation
docs.text: RQ2 is not just “requirements moved downward.” T22 supports a more precise mechanism story: the market is producing more aspirational, template-like, multi-constraint postings, especially around AI and scope. The evidence for literal ghost requirements exists, but it is not the dominant phenomenon.

docs.title: Emerging narrative
docs.text: The cleanest framing is that SWE hiring language is becoming more bundled and more aspirational, with AI terms used in ways that look more like signaling and templating than a one-to-one screening requirement. The direct-vs-aggregator split is real, but it is secondary to the broader template/aspiration shift.

docs.title: Research question evolution
docs.text:
Keep RQ2, but sharpen it to distinguish:

RQ2a: Which requirements expanded in bundled form?

RQ2b: Which AI and scope mentions are aspirational or template-like rather than hard screening requirements?

That is more faithful to the evidence than a simple migration narrative.

docs.title: Gaps and weaknesses
docs.text:
• The primary frame is only 3,983 section-filtered LLM rows, so thin cells are a real constraint.
  • Template saturation is still a heuristic. It is good for ranking companies, not for causal claims.
  • The ghost score should not be read as a latent variable. It is a ranking device for inspection.

docs.title: Direction for next wave
docs.text: Use T22 as a validity check when discussing scope and AI in the synthesis: AI is real, but it is also more aspirational and more template-sensitive than the strongest version of the demand story would imply.

docs.title: Current paper positioning
docs.text: T22 strengthens the paper’s methods-validity backbone. The contribution is not “we found ghost jobs.” The contribution is that the posting language itself is shifting toward more aspirational, multi-constraint templates, and that shift has to be controlled before any labor-market interpretation.
